questions.questionText:
A  14-year-old  girl,  who  has  received  three  courses  of  chemotherapy  for  Hodgkin  disease,  is  brought  to  the  office  by  
her father because of worsening cough, shortness of breath, a low-grade fever and fatigue for the past 2 days. No one 
at  home  is  ill.  She  says  she  is  taking  no  medications.  Her  vaccinations  are  up-to-date.  She  attends  a  private  school.  
Vital  signs  are  temperature  37.1°C  (98.8°F),  pulse  100/min,  respirations  35/min,  and  blood  pressure  110/72  mm  Hg.  
Oxygen  saturation  on  pulse  oximetry  is  87%  while  breathing  room  air.  Physical  examination  discloses  a  few  coarse  
breath  sounds  in  the  lower  lobes  and  retractions.  Chest  x-ray  shows  no  masses  and  a  mild  interstitial  pattern.  No  
infiltrates or effusions are noted and heart size is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate step at this 
time?

choices.A: Admit the girl to the hospital for high-dose trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole therapy
choices.B: Begin isoniazid therapy in the girl and test the family for tuberculosis
choices.C: Prescribe ciprofloxacin and schedule a return visit for tomorrow
choices.D: Prescribe oral fluconazole for the girl

explanations.A: This immunocompromised patient (post-chemotherapy for Hodgkin disease) with interstitial infiltrates, hypoxia (O2 sat 87%), and respiratory distress has a classic presentation of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP). High-dose IV trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) is the treatment of choice for PCP, and hospital admission is required given the severity of hypoxia and respiratory compromise.
explanations.B: The presentation (interstitial pattern without consolidation, immunocompromised host, absence of TB exposure history or classic risk factors) is not consistent with tuberculosis. TB typically presents with upper lobe cavitary lesions or consolidation, and initiating TB therapy without diagnostic confirmation would be inappropriate here.
explanations.C: Ciprofloxacin covers typical and some atypical bacterial pathogens but has no activity against Pneumocystis jirovecii, the most likely pathogen in this immunocompromised child with interstitial pneumonia and hypoxia. Outpatient management with a fluoroquinolone would be unsafe given her oxygen saturation of 87%.
explanations.D: Oral fluconazole is used for fungal infections such as candidiasis or cryptococcosis, not for the diffuse interstitial pattern seen here which is characteristic of PCP. This would be an inappropriate empiric choice in a chemotherapy patient with this clinical and radiographic picture.
explanations.E: Lung biopsy is an invasive procedure and is not indicated as the initial step when the clinical presentation strongly suggests PCP in an immunocompromised patient. Empiric treatment with TMP-SMX should be initiated promptly; BAL or biopsy can be reserved for cases that fail to respond to empiric therapy.

questions.questionText:
A  14-year-old  African  American  girl  comes  to  the  office  for  the  first  time  for  a  sports  physical  examination.  She  is  
asymptomatic.  Her  medical  history  is  unremarkable.  Family  history  shows  that  her  maternal  grandmother  had  a  
myocardial  infarction  at  age  65  years.  Her  mother  has  hypertension  that  is  well  controlled  with  antihypertensive  
medication.  Vital  signs  are  temperature  37.2°C  (99.0°F),  pulse  86/min,  respirations  16/min,  and  blood  pressure  
112/74 mm Hg.  Height  is  165 cm (5 ft 5 in; 75th percentile)  and  weight  is  45 kg (99 lb; 25th percentile).  Physical 
examination shows a grade 2–3/6 systolic heart murmur at the left sternal border. The second heart sound (S

) is loud 
with fixed splitting and is heard best in the second left intercostal space. The family is anxious and requests an answer 
regarding  the  patient's  sports  participation.  Which  of  the  following  is  the  most  appropriate  advice  to  give  the  patient  
and her parents?

choices.A: Allow her to participate in all sports
choices.B: Allow her to participate in noncontact sports only
choices.C: Allow her to participate in sports but recommend cardiac catheterization
choices.D: Allow her to participate in sports but recommend echocardiography
choices.E: Prescribe prophylactic antibiotics for use prior to each game

explanations.A: Allowing unrestricted sports participation without further workup is inappropriate here because the fixed splitting of S2 and the systolic murmur are classic findings of an atrial septal defect (ASD), which requires echocardiographic evaluation before clearance. ASDs can cause right heart volume overload and arrhythmias that may be worsened by strenuous athletic activity.
explanations.B: There is no evidence that contact sports specifically pose greater risk than noncontact sports in ASD; the key issue is characterizing the defect and the degree of shunting before making activity recommendations. Restricting only contact sports does not address the need for diagnostic clarification.
explanations.C: Cardiac catheterization is a more invasive procedure and is not the appropriate first step when a noninvasive test (echocardiography) can characterize the defect. Catheterization may ultimately be needed for hemodynamic assessment or intervention, but echocardiography comes first.
explanations.D: The fixed splitting of S2 with a systolic ejection murmur at the left sternal border is the hallmark of an atrial septal defect. Echocardiography is the noninvasive first-line test to confirm the diagnosis, assess shunt size and direction, and evaluate right heart dimensions before making sports participation recommendations.
explanations.E: Antibiotic prophylaxis for sports is not indicated for ASD. Current guidelines (2007 AHA) reserve infective endocarditis prophylaxis for high-risk conditions such as prosthetic valves or prior endocarditis; isolated ASD is not among them. Prophylaxis would not address the underlying hemodynamic concern.

questions.setVignette:
An 81-year-old woman is being prepared for discharge following a 3-week stay in the hospital for repair of a fractured hip 
that  she  sustained  while  gardening.  She  now  ambulates  with  difficulty  using  a  walker,  but  she  is  determined  to  become  
independent  again  and  to  return  to  her  own  home.  Her  daughter,  who  is  in  the  room  with  the  patient,  says,  "I  want  to  take  
Mother home with me because I'm concerned that she could fall and break her hip again. Mom says she doesn't really want to 
leave her own home, but she will do what is best." The daughter turns to her mother and says firmly, "Isn't that right, Mom?" 
The mother says, "Yes, I guess so," averting eye contact with both her daughter and you by looking down toward the floor.
questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most appropriate response to the mother?

choices.A: "Are you sure you want to go home with your daughter?"
choices.B: "How would you feel if you fell again and had another fracture?"
choices.C: "Is this really your decision or is it your daughter's?"
choices.D: "I would like to talk with you in private now."
choices.E: "You are lucky to have a daughter who wants to take care of you."

explanations.A: This response questions the patient's stated preference in a dismissive tone without first assessing whether the patient is expressing her own wishes freely or is being coerced. It could undermine the therapeutic relationship and does not create a safe, private space for the patient to speak openly.
explanations.B: Asking "How would you feel if you fell again?" is a fear-inducing, leading question that does not respect the patient's autonomy or open a dialogue about her concerns. It may come across as paternalistic and does not allow the patient to disclose potential concerns about her home situation.
explanations.C: This response questions the patient's decisional authority in a way that could be perceived as accusatory toward the daughter without gathering more information. It assumes coercion without first assessing the situation and may damage trust.
explanations.D: Speaking with the patient privately is the most appropriate first step when there is a possibility of elder abuse, coercion, or that the patient's stated preferences may not reflect her true wishes. A private conversation allows the physician to assess decision-making capacity, identify any coercion, and discuss options in a safe setting.
explanations.E: This statement is dismissive and could inadvertently reinforce a potentially coercive dynamic. It does not address the physician's responsibility to ensure that the elderly patient's preferences and safety are being respected, and it closes off further inquiry.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is most likely to prevent another fall and a possible fracture in this patient?

choices.A: Advise her to walk only when accompanied by an adult
choices.B: Ensure that she does not have orthostatic hypotension
choices.C: Provide her with assistance for activities of daily living
choices.D: Provide her with an electric wheelchair
choices.E: Request a visiting nurse to assess the safety of her living environment

explanations.A: Advising the patient to walk only when accompanied restricts her independence and autonomy without addressing the underlying causes of falls. It is not evidence-based as a primary fall prevention strategy and does not address environmental hazards, medication issues, or functional limitations.
explanations.B: While orthostatic hypotension is an important and reversible cause of falls in the elderly and should be evaluated, it is not the single most important intervention for this patient who is returning home. A home safety assessment addresses the broader range of environmental fall risk factors.
explanations.C: Providing assistance with activities of daily living (ADLs) is supportive but does not specifically target fall prevention. Many falls occur during independent activity in the home environment, so modifying that environment is more directly preventive.
explanations.D: An electric wheelchair would further reduce mobility and independence and is not appropriate for a patient who is ambulating with a walker and is determined to regain independence. It does not address fall risk and may actually worsen functional decline.
explanations.E: A visiting nurse home safety assessment is the most evidence-based intervention for preventing falls in elderly patients returning home after hospitalization. It identifies and allows correction of specific environmental hazards (loose rugs, poor lighting, bathroom grab bars) that are the most common contributors to falls in this setting.

questions.questionText:
A  27-year-old  Mexican  woman,  gravida  4,  para  2,  aborta  1,  who  is  at  25  weeks'  gestation,  comes  to  the  emergency  
department  because  of  painless  vaginal  bleeding  that  she  noticed  2  hours  ago  after  sexual  intercourse  with  her  
husband.  The  patient  has  not  received  routine  prenatal  care  during  her  pregnancy,  though  she  was  treated  for  
chlamydia  with  azithromycin  at  15  weeks'  gestation.  She  gave  birth  to  both  of  her  daughters  at  home  in  Mexico  via  
normal vaginal delivery. She had one spontaneous abortion at 5 weeks' gestation. Vital signs are temperature 37.0°C 
(98.6°F), pulse 100/min, respirations 24/min, and blood pressure 110/64 mm Hg. Sterile speculum examination shows 
4  mL  of  blood  in  the  vaginal  vault.  The  cervix  appears  closed.  Ultrasonography  of  the  pelvis  is  most  likely  to  show  
which of the following causes of bleeding in this patient?

explanations.A: Cervicitis can cause vaginal bleeding, but the cervix appears closed on speculum exam and the patient was already treated for chlamydia at 15 weeks. Cervicitis typically does not cause significant bleeding provoked by intercourse in the second trimester, and ultrasonography would not confirm cervicitis as the cause.
explanations.B: Placenta accreta (abnormal placental invasion into the myometrium) is associated with prior uterine surgery or curettage, not with this patient's history. It typically presents with hemorrhage at delivery and would not be the ultrasound finding explaining antepartum painless bleeding at 25 weeks.
explanations.C: Placenta previa (placenta overlying or near the internal cervical os) is the classic cause of painless vaginal bleeding in the second and third trimesters, particularly triggered by intercourse. This patient's presentation — painless bleeding after intercourse with a closed cervix — is the textbook presentation of placenta previa, which ultrasonography will confirm.
explanations.D: Placental abruption typically presents with painful vaginal bleeding and uterine tenderness, not painless bleeding with a closed cervix. This patient has no abdominal pain, and her vital signs are stable without the uterine rigidity characteristic of abruption.
explanations.E: Preterm labor presents with uterine contractions and cervical change; the closed cervix on examination and the absence of contractions make this diagnosis unlikely. Ultrasonography would show cervical shortening in preterm labor, not the placental location that explains painless antepartum bleeding.

questions.questionText:
An 80-year-old African American woman is brought to the office for the first time by her son because she has signs of 
mildly decreasing mental function. She is having increasing trouble reading, writing, and watching television. She has 
mild,  stable  angina  pectoris  and  she  had  an  uncomplicated  myocardial  infarction  8  years  ago.  Physical  examination  
discloses no abnormalities except for corrected visual acuity of 20/200 O.U., which appears to be caused by cataracts. 
Mini-Mental State examination score is 29/30. Which of the following is the most correct statement about this patient's 
condition?

choices.A: Her daily activities would likely improve if she had cataract extraction with lens implantation
choices.B: Her diminished mental status is a contraindication for a cataract operation
choices.C: Her history of cardiac disease and advanced age are contraindications for a cataract operation
choices.D: Her mental status should be reevaluated in 1 year
choices.E: You need more information to decide whether she would be helped by a cataract operation

explanations.A: This patient's diminished vision (20/200) is attributed to cataracts, and her Mini-Mental Status score of 29/30 indicates near-normal cognitive function. Cataract extraction with lens implantation is a highly effective, low-risk outpatient procedure that would restore visual acuity and directly improve her ability to read, write, and watch television, markedly improving her daily functioning.
explanations.B: A Mini-Mental Status score of 29/30 indicates only very minimal cognitive impairment (likely related to her vision, not true dementia), and this does not represent a contraindication to cataract surgery. The cognitive findings here are not significant enough to withhold a beneficial low-risk procedure.
explanations.C: Stable angina and prior MI are not contraindications to cataract surgery, which is typically performed under topical or local anesthesia with minimal systemic stress. Advanced age alone is also not a contraindication; the procedure is highly beneficial in elderly patients.
explanations.D: Delaying reevaluation for 1 year is inappropriate when the cause of her functional decline (cataracts causing 20/200 vision) has already been identified and an effective treatment is available. Watchful waiting in this scenario would allow preventable disability to persist.
explanations.E: Sufficient information is already available: the cause of visual impairment is cataracts identified on examination, cognitive status is near normal, and her cardiac history is stable. No additional information is required to recommend surgical correction.

questions.questionText:
A 70-year-old Vietnamese fisherman is brought to the emergency department by his son, who says that his father has 
had  sweats  occurring  twice  a  night  soaking  his  bedclothes,  and  a  cough  productive  of  yellow,  foul-tasting  fluid  and  
blood-tinged sputum for the past 3 weeks. His appetite has been poor for 2 months and he has lost 12 kg (26 lb) during 
that time. Physical examination shows an emaciated man in acute distress; he is coughing frequently and deeply. Vital 
signs are temperature 37.8°C (100.0°F), pulse 98/min, and blood pressure 105/60 mm Hg. His respirations are shallow 
but not labored; loud rhonchi are heard on the right side of the chest. His liver and spleen are palpable and nontender. 
His inguinal nodes are matted but nontender. Chest x-ray is shown. Sputum smear discloses multiple neutrophils but 
normal flora. Appropriate diagnostic tests are completed to confirm the diagnosis. Which of the following is the most 
appropriate management at this time?
questions.imageAlt: Chest X-ray showing bilateral upper lobe infiltrates with cavitation

choices.E: Referral to a thoracic surgeon for right upper lobectomy

explanations.A: The clinical picture — an elderly Southeast Asian man with chronic productive cough, hemoptysis, night sweats, significant weight loss, and upper lobe pathology on chest X-ray — is not consistent with a routine bacterial pneumonia requiring standard antibiotics. Antibiotics would fail to address the underlying mycobacterial infection.
explanations.B: Antifungal therapy would be appropriate for pulmonary fungal infections (e.g., histoplasmosis, aspergillosis), but the clinical and radiographic picture in this Vietnamese patient is overwhelmingly consistent with tuberculosis. Sputum cultures have already been sent, and empiric antifungal therapy is not indicated.
explanations.C: This patient's presentation is classic for pulmonary tuberculosis: an elderly Vietnamese immigrant (high-prevalence population) with chronic hemoptysis, night sweats, weight loss, matted nontender lymph nodes, and chest X-ray showing bilateral upper lobe infiltrates with cavitation. Antimycobacterial therapy (RIPE: rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) is the appropriate treatment.
explanations.D: Bronchoscopy may be used to obtain specimens when sputum smears are non-diagnostic, but the question states that appropriate diagnostic tests have been completed to confirm the diagnosis. Proceeding directly to treatment is the correct step once TB is confirmed.
explanations.E: Surgical lobectomy is reserved for rare cases of TB with drug resistance, massive hemoptysis, or destroyed lung that fails medical therapy. Initial treatment is always medical (antimycobacterial therapy), and surgery is not a first-line management option.

questions.questionText:
A  50-year-old  man  comes  to  the  emergency  department  because  of  a  2-hour  history  of  vomiting  "coffee-ground"  
material. He has lost 4.5 kg (10 lb) in the past 6 months. Dark blood is obtained on passing a nasogastric tube. Which 
of the following is the most important factor in determining this patient's long-term prognosis?

choices.A: Amount and rate of blood loss
choices.E: Initial response to a bolus of saline

explanations.A: While the amount and rate of blood loss determine immediate hemodynamic stability and guide acute resuscitation, they do not predict long-term prognosis once the patient is stabilized. The underlying etiology is far more important for determining survival and the risk of re-bleeding.
explanations.B: The cause of upper GI bleeding is the most important determinant of long-term prognosis because it determines the risk of re-bleeding, the treatment strategy, and underlying mortality. For example, variceal bleeding from cirrhosis carries far worse prognosis than bleeding from a peptic ulcer, while malignancy-related bleeding has its own implications.
explanations.C: A prior history of GI bleeding suggests a chronic or recurrent condition but does not independently determine long-term outcome as powerfully as the underlying etiology. The cause of bleeding drives both treatment decisions and prognosis.
explanations.D: Initial hematocrit reflects blood loss at presentation but lags behind acute hemorrhage. It is valuable for acute management decisions but does not independently determine long-term prognosis, which is driven by the underlying diagnosis.
explanations.E: Response to initial saline bolus reflects hemodynamic reserve and helps predict immediate risk of circulatory collapse, but it is a short-term indicator. Long-term prognosis is determined by the etiology of bleeding, not the initial fluid resuscitation response.

questions.setVignette:
A 22-year-old African American woman, gravida 1, para 1, is evaluated in the maternity ward of the hospital 24 hours after 
giving birth to a 4267-g (9-lb 7-oz) male infant via uncomplicated vaginal delivery. Following delivery, she underwent repair 
of a fourth-degree perineal laceration. She is currently able to walk to the bathroom and void without difficulty, but she has 
not had a bowel movement since delivery. Medical history is otherwise unremarkable and her only medications are prenatal 
vitamins. Vital signs are temperature 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse 68/min, respirations 18/min, and blood pressure 128/86 mm Hg. 
The uterus is palpable to the level of the umbilicus. The vaginal laceration is not inflamed or swollen. Sutures are intact and 
there  is  no  drainage  from  the  site.  Lochia  is  normal.  Hemoglobin  concentration  is  10.8  g/dL.  The  patient  states  that  she  is  
concerned about her insurance company requirement that she stay in the hospital no longer than 48 hours post partum. She is 
worried that she will not be ready to leave tomorrow, as she is breast-feeding and needs more help from the nurses. She also 
is concerned about her bowel function and wants to stay until she is sure it will be normal. She asks if you would extend her 
stay to 72 hours post partum if she is not ready to leave tomorrow.
questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most appropriate response to her request?

choices.A:
"I'm sure the insurance company will understand if you need another day, so you take whatever time you need 
before you go home."
choices.B: "Let me decide whether or not it is too soon for you to leave the hospital."
choices.C: "Let's see how you feel tomorrow and we can discuss the most appropriate time to leave then."
choices.D: "You concentrate on getting better and leave the insurance company to me."
choices.E: "Unfortunately I have no control over the insurance company, so you had better plan on leaving tomorrow."

explanations.A: Reassuring the patient that the insurance company will make exceptions is misleading and potentially inaccurate; the physician cannot make promises about insurance policy decisions. This response also fails to engage with the patient's clinical status and her preferences regarding timing of discharge.
explanations.B: Telling the patient that the physician will decide when she leaves is paternalistic and does not respect patient autonomy. Discharge planning should be a collaborative process that incorporates both clinical readiness and the patient's own preferences and concerns.
explanations.C: Deferring the discussion to the next day acknowledges both the patient's concerns and the physician's need to assess her clinical status over time. It respects her autonomy while maintaining clinical judgment, and it avoids making premature promises about discharge timing — the most appropriate and balanced response.
explanations.D: Telling the patient to leave the insurance issue to the physician is unrealistic and avoids the need to engage with her specific concerns. It also fails to set appropriate expectations about what the physician can and cannot do regarding insurance decisions.
explanations.E: Telling the patient she "had better plan" on leaving removes her agency and frames the discharge as dictated solely by the insurance company, which is neither accurate nor therapeutic. It does not engage with her clinical status or address her concerns empathetically.

questions.questionText:
Which  of  the  following  would  be  the  most  important  indication  for  extending  this  patient's  hospital  stay  beyond  
48 hours post partum?

choices.A: Abdominal distention and lack of appetite at 48 hours post partum
choices.B: Lack of bowel movement post partum
choices.C: Need for nursing assistance with breast-feeding
choices.D: Palpation of the uterus above the pubic symphysis for more than 48 hours post partum
choices.E: Persistence of lochia for more than 24 hours post partum

explanations.A: Abdominal distention and lack of appetite at 48 hours postpartum may indicate an ileus or other GI complication, particularly relevant in this patient who had a fourth-degree perineal laceration repair. These signs suggest a surgical or post-procedural complication that would justify extending the hospital stay for further evaluation and management.
explanations.B: Lack of a bowel movement in the first 48 hours postpartum is common and expected, especially after perineal repair. It is not an indication to extend hospitalization unless accompanied by other symptoms such as distention or pain.
explanations.C: Breastfeeding assistance is important but can be provided on an outpatient basis through lactation consultants. It is not a medical indication for extending inpatient stay beyond 48 hours.
explanations.D: The uterine fundus should be at the umbilicus immediately after delivery and involutes at approximately 1 cm/day; it is expected to be palpable above the symphysis for several days postpartum. This is a normal physiologic finding and not an indication to extend hospitalization.
explanations.E: Lochia (postpartum vaginal discharge) normally persists for up to 4-6 weeks after delivery; its presence at 24 hours is entirely expected and normal. It does not represent a pathological finding requiring extended hospitalization.

questions.questionText:
A 13-month-old child is brought to the emergency department because of urticaria, swelling of the lips, and difficulty 
breathing immediately after eating an egg. A potential risk for hypersensitivity reaction is posed by vaccination against 
which of the following illnesses?

explanations.A: Hepatitis A and B vaccines are not grown in egg-based media and do not contain egg protein. They are safe to administer to egg-allergic individuals without precaution.
explanations.B: Influenza vaccines are traditionally manufactured using embryonated chicken eggs and may contain residual egg protein (ovalbumin). In patients with known egg allergy (particularly those who have had anaphylactic reactions, as this child did after eating egg), influenza vaccination requires careful consideration and may need to be given in a medical setting with monitoring.
explanations.C: The pertussis vaccine (DTaP/Tdap) is not produced in eggs and contains no egg-derived proteins. Egg allergy does not pose a risk for hypersensitivity reactions to pertussis vaccination.
explanations.D: The inactivated polio vaccine (IPV) is grown in Vero cells (monkey kidney cells), not in eggs. It does not contain egg protein and is safe in egg-allergic patients.
explanations.E: The typhoid vaccine (both oral Ty21a and injectable polysaccharide) is not egg-derived and does not pose a hypersensitivity risk in egg-allergic patients.

questions.questionText:
A  42-year-old  white  man  is  brought  to  the  emergency  department  by  his  same  sex  partner  because  of  confusion,  
diplopia, and mild weakness of his right arm. The patient is somewhat agitated and shows confusion for recent events. 
Temperature is 38.3°C (101.0°F). There is decreased pupillary response on the left with paresis of lateral gaze on the 
right. Peripheral leukocyte count is increased. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation of 
this patient's neurologic signs and symptoms?

choices.D: Lumbar puncture for examination of cerebrospinal fluid

explanations.A: Bilateral carotid arteriography would be appropriate to evaluate for carotid stenosis or dissection in the setting of stroke, but this patient has fever and signs of elevated intracranial pressure (confusion, focal neurologic deficits) that require structural brain imaging first. Arteriography does not evaluate for mass lesions or abscesses.
explanations.B: In a patient with focal neurologic deficits (lateral gaze palsy, pupillary asymmetry), altered mental status, and fever, CT of the head is the essential first step before lumbar puncture to rule out a space-occupying lesion or herniation that would make lumbar puncture dangerous. In the MSM context, CNS lymphoma or toxoplasma abscess must be excluded.
explanations.C: EEG is useful for evaluating seizure disorders but is not the priority in a patient with fever, focal neurologic deficits, and altered mental status. The focal findings suggest a structural lesion or focal encephalitis, not a primary seizure disorder.
explanations.D: Lumbar puncture is critical for diagnosing meningitis or encephalitis but must be preceded by CT of the head when there are focal neurologic deficits, to exclude a mass lesion or elevated intracranial pressure that would make LP dangerous. Performing LP first could precipitate transtentorial herniation.
explanations.E: HIV testing is important in this clinical context (MSM with fever and neurologic findings) but does not address the acute neurologic emergency. Knowing the HIV status will not change the immediate management, which requires structural imaging to guide safe diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

questions.questionText:
A  35-year-old  man  is  brought  to  the  emergency  department  because  of  altered  mental  status.  He  is  disoriented  and  
complains  about  his  vision.  You  have  been  his  physician  for  the  past  3  years.  He  has  type  1  diabetes  mellitus  and  a  
known history of intravenous drug abuse. You last saw him 2 weeks ago; at that visit his serum glucose concentration 
was  150  mg/dL  3  hours  after  eating.  Today,  vital  signs  are  temperature  38.1°C  (100.5°F),  pulse  110/min,  and  blood  
pressure  190/70  mm  Hg.  On  physical  examination  pupils  are  constricted;  funduscopic  examination  of  the  left  eye  
following dilation is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate test at this time?
questions.imageAlt: Fundoscopic image showing flame-shaped hemorrhages and hard exudates

explanations.A: This IV drug user presents with fever, hypertension, altered mental status, and fundoscopic findings consistent with septic emboli (Roth spots, hemorrhages). In an IV drug abuser, infective endocarditis is the leading concern, and blood cultures are the essential first step to confirm bacteremia and identify the causative organism before initiating antibiotic therapy.
explanations.B: Chest X-ray may be useful to look for septic pulmonary emboli (a complication of right-sided endocarditis) but is not the most critical diagnostic test at this moment. The fundoscopic findings and fever in an IV drug user point to bacteremia/endocarditis, for which blood cultures are the priority.
explanations.C: Hemoglobin A1c reflects glycemic control over the preceding 2-3 months and is not relevant to this acute presentation. The patient's prior glucose was only mildly elevated, and the acute neurologic and ocular findings are not explained by chronic hyperglycemia.
explanations.D: HIV antibody testing is important for this patient given his IV drug use history but does not address the acute emergency. HIV does not explain the acute fever, hypertension, and retinal findings, which are more consistent with septic emboli from bacterial endocarditis.
explanations.E: Plasma renin activity is used to evaluate secondary hypertension (e.g., renovascular hypertension) and is not relevant to this acute presentation of probable infective endocarditis with systemic embolization.

questions.questionText:
A 38-year-old white letter carrier returns to the office for follow-up of abnormal results of a liver chemistry profile ordered 3 weeks ago during a routine examination. At that time, physical examination disclosed no abnormalities, but serum AST concentration was 72 U/L. Serum bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase concentrations were within the reference ranges. Medical history is significant for an episode of hepatitis A at age 22 years. He has no history of transfusions or intravenous drug use. He drinks two to three beers daily. Today's follow-up laboratory study results are shown: 
Serum
  Anti-HAV    Positive
  Anti-HBs    Negative
  HBsAg       Positive
  HBeAg       Positive
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.C: Instruct him to cease alcohol consumption and retest him in 2 months
choices.D: Order hepatitis B virus polymerase chain reaction test

explanations.A: Interferon-alfa (with or without ribavirin) is indicated for chronic active hepatitis B with evidence of viral replication and liver inflammation, but this patient has only mildly elevated AST and alcohol use as a confounding factor. The first step is to eliminate confounders (alcohol) and reassess before initiating antiviral therapy.
explanations.B: Corticosteroids are contraindicated in chronic hepatitis B infection. They can precipitate a flare of hepatitis B by suppressing immune control of viral replication, leading to potentially life-threatening hepatic decompensation.
explanations.C: This patient drinks 2-3 beers daily and has HBsAg and HBeAg positive status. Alcohol is hepatotoxic and can independently elevate AST, confounding the picture. The most appropriate next step is to eliminate alcohol and repeat liver tests in 2 months to determine whether the enzyme elevation is truly from viral hepatitis or alcohol before making treatment decisions.
explanations.D: HBV PCR (viral load) is useful for monitoring treatment response and guiding antiviral therapy decisions, but is premature as the first next step when alcohol confounds the enzyme elevation. Eliminating alcohol and rechecking liver enzymes should precede further virologic workup.
explanations.E: Liver biopsy provides histologic grading of inflammation and fibrosis, which ultimately guides treatment decisions, but is an invasive procedure not warranted as the immediate next step when a reversible confounder (alcohol) has not yet been addressed.

questions.questionText:
A  75-year-old  white  woman  returns  to  the  office  after  6  months  of  missed  appointments.  She  says  she  is  feeling  
depressed. You have been treating her for years for a variety of disorders, including bipolar disorder, hypothyroidism, 
atrial fibrillation, peptic ulcer disease, and hypertension. She takes daily lithium carbonate, levothyroxine, haloperidol, 
sertraline, benztropine, digoxin, propranolol, ranitidine, and warfarin. She says, "I'm doing fine except for shakiness in 
my hands." She also says her mood is "a little depressed." She has no hallucinations or delusions. Vital signs are pulse 
78/min  with  an  irregularly  irregular  rhythm,  and  blood  pressure  160/95  mm  Hg.  Physical  examination  shows  a  fine  
tremor  of  the  hands  when  they  are  extended.  On  memory  testing,  she  recalls  one  of  three  objects  after  2  minutes.  
Which of the following is the most likely cause of the patient's depression?

explanations.A: Benztropine is an anticholinergic used to treat extrapyramidal side effects of antipsychotics. While anticholinergics can cause cognitive effects, benztropine is not a well-recognized cause of clinical depression.
explanations.B: Digoxin at toxic levels can cause neuropsychiatric effects but is not a classic cause of depression. This patient's irregular pulse and AF suggest digoxin is being used for rate control; the primary psychiatric side effect to monitor with digoxin toxicity would be visual disturbances and confusion, not depression.
explanations.C: Haloperidol is a typical antipsychotic used here for bipolar disorder. While it can cause sedation and extrapyramidal effects (which may mimic depression), it is not the most recognized pharmacological cause of depressive symptoms compared to beta-blockers.
explanations.D: Propranolol is a nonselective beta-blocker with well-documented CNS penetration. It is a classic cause of depression, fatigue, and sexual dysfunction due to its lipophilic nature and central noradrenergic and serotonergic effects. It is the most likely culprit among this patient's medications.
explanations.E: Ranitidine (H2 blocker) has rarely been associated with CNS effects in elderly patients but is not a well-established cause of clinical depression. It is far less likely than propranolol to be causing her depressive symptoms.

questions.questionText:
A  68-year-old  man  is  in  the  hospital  because  he  requires  mechanical  ventilation  for  an  exacerbation  of  chronic  
obstructive pulmonary disease. On the second day after admission he developed a pneumothorax on the right side that 
required tube thoracostomy. An air leak is noted for the next 24 hours, which now has stopped. However, the patient 
has become restless and combative. Breath sounds are diminished in the right side of the chest and the patient now has 
tachycardia.  Blood  pressure  is  130/80  mm  Hg.  After  ordering  a  
STAT  portable  x-ray  of  the  chest,  which  of  the  
following is the most appropriate step?

choices.A: Add 4 cm of positive end-expiratory pressure
choices.D: Remove the patient from the ventilator and ventilate him with a bag-valve mask

explanations.A: Adding positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) increases intrathoracic pressure and would worsen a tension pneumothorax or re-collapsed lung, potentially increasing the risk of contralateral barotrauma. This is not an appropriate first step in a patient with likely chest tube failure.
explanations.B: Beta-blockers would address tachycardia but do not treat the underlying problem. The tachycardia here is likely compensatory due to respiratory compromise or re-accumulation of pneumothorax, not a primary cardiac dysrhythmia requiring pharmacologic rate control.
explanations.C: Alprazolam (a benzodiazepine) would sedate the patient and potentially worsen respiratory function, masking the signs of a true respiratory emergency. Agitation and combativeness in this context are signs of hypoxia and should be treated by addressing the cause, not with sedation.
explanations.D: Removing the patient from the ventilator and bag-valve mask ventilation is appropriate for certain ventilator-related emergencies (e.g., suspected auto-PEEP or tension pneumothorax), but in this case the chest tube is already in place and the most likely problem is tube malposition. Repositioning the tube is the more targeted intervention.
explanations.E: The cessation of air leak followed by return of diminished breath sounds, tachycardia, and agitation indicates the chest tube has likely become occluded or malpositioned, allowing re-accumulation of the pneumothorax. Repositioning or replacing the chest tube directly addresses the cause of deterioration and is the most appropriate immediate intervention.

questions.questionText:
A 36-year-old man comes to the office because of headaches that began 2 weeks  ago.  The  headaches  are  moderately  
severe,  are  present  when  he  awakens  in  the  morning,  and  are  relieved  with  over-the-counter  analgesics.  He  has  no  
prior history of headaches. He tells you he was promoted to an upper-level managerial position in his accounting firm 
about  8  months  ago,  which  necessitated  relocating.  Physical  examination  now  discloses  no  abnormalities  except  for  
blurring of the optic disc margins bilaterally. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.A: Begin a trial of a β-blocking medication
choices.B: Order CT scan of the head
choices.D: Refer him for consultation with a neurologist
choices.E: Refer him for consultation with a neurosurgeon

explanations.A: Beta-blockers are used for migraine prophylaxis, but this patient's headaches with papilledema (blurred optic disc margins bilaterally) are a red flag for increased intracranial pressure requiring urgent structural evaluation. Treating presumptively with migraine prophylaxis without ruling out a mass lesion would be dangerous.
explanations.B: Bilateral papilledema (blurring of the optic disc margins) in a patient with new-onset morning headaches is a classic sign of increased intracranial pressure, which may be caused by a brain tumor, pseudotumor cerebri, or other space-occupying lesion. CT of the head is the essential next step to identify the etiology before any other management.
explanations.C: EEG is used to evaluate seizure disorders and is not indicated as the initial test for increased intracranial pressure with papilledema. It would not identify a structural cause of elevated ICP.
explanations.D: Neurology consultation may ultimately be needed, but the most urgent priority is structural imaging to identify or exclude a brain tumor or other intracranial pathology. Referral should follow, not precede, CT imaging.
explanations.E: Neurosurgical referral is premature without first obtaining imaging to identify whether there is actually a surgical lesion. CT of the head must be performed first to guide appropriate specialist referral.

questions.questionText:
A 16-year-old high school student, whose prenatal course you have managed, gave birth to a 3256-g (7-lb 3-oz) baby 
girl during the night with the assistance of your associate. On morning rounds you note that the delivery records report 
that she had mildly elevated blood pressure during labor and sustained an estimated third-stage blood loss of 500 mL. 
Today  blood  pressure  is  132/84  mm  Hg,  she  is  afebrile,  and  deep  tendon  reflexes  are  normal.  The  uterine  fundus  is  
firm and at the level of the umbilicus, and her perineum is slightly edematous. Hematocrit is 33%. She is cuddling her 
infant  and  normal  bonding  seems  to  be  occurring.  Which  of  the  following  is  the  most  important  next  step  in  
management?

explanations.A: While a hematocrit of 33% indicates mild anemia (consistent with the estimated 500 mL blood loss), this is not the immediate priority. Iron supplementation can be initiated but is a secondary concern compared to the critical educational need of this 16-year-old first-time mother.
explanations.B: The blood pressure of 132/84 mm Hg with normal deep tendon reflexes and absence of other signs of preeclampsia in the postpartum period does not require antihypertensive therapy. Mild blood pressure elevation postpartum is common and typically self-resolving; methyldopa is used for antepartum hypertension management.
explanations.C: The uterine fundus is firm and at the umbilicus, which is a normal finding at this time postpartum. Fundal massage is indicated for a boggy uterus to prevent or treat uterine atony and postpartum hemorrhage; it is unnecessary here and would be uncomfortable without clinical indication.
explanations.D: Sitz baths are helpful for perineal comfort and hygiene and should be offered, but they are not the most important next step. This addresses a minor comfort measure rather than the critical clinical priority.
explanations.E: This is a 16-year-old adolescent mother who is bonding with her newborn but is a first-time parent who needs education about newborn care, feeding, safe sleep, immunizations, and follow-up. Well-baby care education is the most important next step as her clinical parameters are stable and the primary need is preparing her for safe infant care at home.

questions.setVignette:
A 55-year-old man who has a long history of alcohol dependence comes to the office for his yearly follow-up visit. He has 
consumed 2 pints of vodka daily for many years and has a past history of blackout episodes during intoxication. Following a 
divorce  from  his  second  wife,  he  voluntarily  "detoxified"  himself,  but  this  was  complicated  by  "rum  fits"  with  tactile  and  
visual hallucinations. His medical history includes hypercholesterolemia controlled by diet, benign prostatic enlargement, and 
hypertension,  for  which  he  takes  an  α-blocking  medication.  During  the  course  of  the  examination,  he  tells  you  his  twin  
brother recently died of colon cancer. It is clear from the conversation that he was very close to his brother and he feels that 
his only support system has left him. You discuss your concerns about his reliance on alcohol as a coping mechanism and ask 
if he would consider accepting help during this difficult time. He agrees.
questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.A: Arrange for referral to a substance abuse treatment program while the patient is in the office
choices.C: Prescribe disulfiram and discuss with him its use and potential withdrawal symptoms
choices.E: Suggest he attend an outpatient substance abuse treatment program in his area

explanations.A: This patient presents expressing a desire to stop drinking and begin treatment; the most appropriate action while he is motivated and in the office is to arrange immediate referral to a substance abuse treatment program. Capitalizing on the patient's expressed readiness by acting now — not later — maximizes the likelihood of successful engagement with treatment.
explanations.B: Diazepam (a benzodiazepine) is used to manage acute alcohol withdrawal symptoms (e.g., seizure prophylaxis), not as a long-term strategy for alcohol dependence treatment. It carries its own risk of misuse and dependence in this population and is not appropriate as the first step here.
explanations.C: Disulfiram (Antabuse) causes an aversive reaction when alcohol is consumed and can be a component of alcohol dependence treatment, but it requires patient motivation, informed consent, and ideally, concurrent psychosocial support. It is not the first-line immediate intervention, and prescribing it as the only step misses the importance of structured treatment.
explanations.D: Alcoholics Anonymous is a valuable peer support resource but is not a substitute for formal substance abuse treatment, especially given the severity of this patient's dependence. Referring to AA alone, without arranging formal treatment, underutilizes the office visit and the patient's current motivation.
explanations.E: Suggesting outpatient treatment rather than arranging it in the office fails to capitalize on the patient's present motivation. The evidence supports that immediate in-office facilitation of referral (rather than vague suggestion) significantly improves treatment entry rates.

questions.questionText:
Which  of  the  following  features  in  this  patient's  history  is  most  closely  associated  with  the  risk  for  morbidity  or  
mortality from alcohol withdrawal?

explanations.A: Lack of social support affects long-term recovery and increases relapse risk but is not the clinical feature most directly associated with morbidity or mortality during acute alcohol withdrawal. Social isolation does not predict the severity of physiologic withdrawal.
explanations.B: Blackout episodes reflect severe intoxication and the extent of alcohol use, but they are associated with heavy drinking behavior rather than directly predicting the severity or lethality of alcohol withdrawal syndrome.
explanations.C: Family history of colon cancer is not relevant to alcohol withdrawal risk. It may be relevant for colorectal cancer screening given the patient's age and drinking history, but has no predictive value for withdrawal severity.
explanations.D: A history of prior alcohol withdrawal seizures is the strongest predictor of severe alcohol withdrawal, including delirium tremens (DTs). The phenomenon of 'kindling' means that each subsequent withdrawal episode tends to be more severe; prior seizures indicate that the patient's nervous system is capable of generating severe physiologic withdrawal, placing them at high risk for life-threatening complications.
explanations.E: Alpha-blocking medications are not associated with increased risk of alcohol withdrawal morbidity or mortality. Beta-blockers can blunt some autonomic symptoms of withdrawal but do not prevent seizures or DTs; however, use of alpha-blockers specifically is not a recognized risk factor.

questions.questionText:
A 29-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because she has had increasingly severe lower abdominal 
pain  and  nausea  for  the  past  2  days.  She  is  sexually  active  and  does  not  use  any  contraception.  Her  last  menstrual  
period  ended  6  days  ago.  Temperature  is  38.3°C  (101.0°F).  Physical  examination  discloses  abdominal  tenderness  in  
the lower quadrants bilaterally with rebound and guarding. Pelvic examination discloses leukorrhea at the cervical os 
and  tenderness  of  the  uterus  to  palpation.  The  adnexal  areas  are  tender  but  no  masses  are  palpable.  Which  of  the  
following is the most appropriate diagnostic study?

explanations.A: In a sexually active young woman with lower abdominal pain, fever, cervical motion tenderness, and leukorrhea, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) is the leading diagnosis. A cervical culture identifies the causative organisms (most commonly Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis), guides antibiotic selection, and is the most appropriate first-line diagnostic study.
explanations.B: Culdocentesis (aspiration of the cul-de-sac) was historically used to detect hemoperitoneum in suspected ectopic pregnancy, but has been largely replaced by ultrasound and serum beta-hCG. It is not indicated here since the last menstrual period ended 6 days ago, making pregnancy less likely.
explanations.C: Laparoscopy is the gold standard for definitive diagnosis of PID but is invasive and not required as the initial diagnostic step. It is reserved for cases where the diagnosis is uncertain after non-invasive evaluation or when there is clinical deterioration despite antibiotic treatment.
explanations.D: Serum beta-hCG is important to exclude ectopic pregnancy in a sexually active woman with lower abdominal pain. However, the last menstrual period ended only 6 days ago, making pregnancy very unlikely. The presentation with leukorrhea and cervical tenderness more strongly suggests PID, making cervical culture the priority.
explanations.E: Pelvic ultrasonography is used to evaluate for tubo-ovarian abscess (a complication of PID), ovarian cysts, or ectopic pregnancy. It is useful if no masses are palpated but the diagnosis of PID is uncertain; however, the most diagnostically specific initial step in suspected PID is cervical culture to identify the pathogen.

questions.questionText:
A  28-year-old  woman  of  Scandinavian  descent  comes  to  the  office  because  of  fatigue,  generalized  weakness,  and  
palpitations. She is divorced and lives with her 4-year-old daughter. Medical history is significant for hyperthyroidism 
and mild ophthalmopathy caused by Graves disease. Before initiating therapy, the patient wants to know what she can 
expect in the future. In advising her about the prognosis, which of the following is the most accurate statement?

choices.A: Graves ophthalmopathy will resolve as thyroid hormone secretion is lowered
choices.B: Malignant degeneration of the thyroid gland is a common complication
choices.C: She will not be able to become pregnant
choices.D: The thyroid will continue to increase in size with any nonsurgical treatment
choices.E: Untreated patients are at increased risk for cardiac arrhythmias

explanations.A: Graves ophthalmopathy (thyroid eye disease) is an autoimmune process driven by TSH receptor antibodies and does not reliably improve with treatment of hyperthyroidism. It follows an independent course and may even worsen with radioiodine therapy; patients should be counseled that eye disease and thyroid disease do not necessarily parallel each other.
explanations.B: Thyroid malignancy is not a common complication of Graves disease. In fact, Graves disease is caused by stimulating TSH receptor antibodies, and while thyroid nodules may coexist, malignant degeneration of the gland is not an expected outcome and would be misleading to tell the patient.
explanations.C: Graves disease can impair fertility due to the effects of hyperthyroidism on the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, but successful treatment of hyperthyroidism typically restores fertility. Telling a patient she cannot become pregnant is inaccurate and would cause unnecessary distress.
explanations.D: With antithyroid drug therapy (methimazole or propylthiouracil), radioiodine, or surgery, thyroid gland enlargement (goiter) does not necessarily continue to grow. Antithyroid drugs reduce thyroid hormone production and may reduce gland size; this statement is factually incorrect.
explanations.E: Untreated hyperthyroidism from Graves disease causes a persistent high-output state that stresses the heart, leading to sinus tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, and eventually high-output heart failure. Cardiac arrhythmias — particularly atrial fibrillation — are a well-recognized and serious complication of untreated or poorly controlled Graves disease.

questions.setVignette:
A 38-year-old woman returns to the office for follow-up of tension headaches, which have not improved with trials of several 
appropriate medications. She has been married to a policeman for the past 6 years and has four children (ages 5 to 12 years). 
When asked if she has been under extra stress, she begins to cry. Bruises are evident on both arms. On further questioning, 
she  says  her  husband  hits  her  whenever  he  is  drunk,  which  is  at  least  2  nights  per  week.  She  says,  "He  is  nice...a  good  
husband when he's sober. But when he drinks, oh he's awful! He accuses me of cheating on him. Last night he said he would 
kill me if I try to leave." Her husband is also your patient.
questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most appropriate intervention?

choices.A: Advise her to leave her home with her children and move in with her relatives
choices.B: Contact her husband's supervisor to discuss recent stress levels on the job
choices.C: Gather more information while remaining neutral, since both the husband and wife are your patients
choices.D: Refer her to a domestic violence program
choices.E: Seek a restraining order against her husband on her behalf

explanations.A: Advising her to leave home is an overreach that removes her autonomy — the physician's role is to support the patient's decision-making, not to make major life decisions for her. Unsolicited directives can damage the therapeutic relationship and may actually increase danger if she is not ready to leave.
explanations.B: Contacting her husband's supervisor violates patient confidentiality and is ethically inappropriate without her explicit consent. This action could endanger her further by alerting the abuser and providing no direct protection.
explanations.C: Remaining neutral when domestic violence is suspected is inadequate and potentially harmful — neutrality can inadvertently normalize abuse and delay needed intervention. The physician's obligation is to the patient's safety, which requires an active, supportive response rather than a passive one.
explanations.D: Referring her to a domestic violence program is correct because it connects her with specialized resources — safety planning, legal advocacy, counseling — designed for survivors of intimate partner violence. This respects her autonomy while ensuring she has expert support and information to make safe choices.
explanations.E: Seeking a restraining order on her behalf without her consent violates her autonomy and can escalate danger — enforcement of a restraining order she did not choose could provoke retaliation from the abuser. Legal steps must be patient-initiated and coordinated with proper advocacy support.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most important question to ask at this time?

choices.A: "Do you think this might be causing your headaches?"
choices.B: "Has your husband also lost his temper with any of the children?"
choices.C: "Have you been drinking at the time of the fights?"
choices.D: "Have you or your husband been receiving any kind of counseling?"
choices.E: "Why have you stayed in this marriage?"

explanations.A: Asking whether the fights are causing her headaches is a leading question that prematurely conflates two issues and does not gather the most critical safety information. While tension headaches may be stress-related, identifying child endangerment takes priority.
explanations.B: Asking whether the husband has also lost his temper with the children is correct because mandatory reporting laws require physicians to assess and report suspected child abuse. Determining whether children in the home are at risk is the most urgent clinical and legal obligation when domestic violence is present.
explanations.C: Asking whether she has been drinking shifts blame onto the victim and is a non-therapeutic, stigmatizing question. Alcohol use by the victim does not cause or excuse intimate partner violence and is not the priority inquiry.
explanations.D: Asking about counseling is a reasonable follow-up question but is not the most important at this moment — the immediate priority is assessing child safety, not exploring prior treatment history. This question can be addressed after mandatory reporting obligations are clarified.
explanations.E: Asking why she has stayed in the marriage is judgmental, victim-blaming, and creates a barrier to disclosure. It implies she bears responsibility for the abuse and is likely to alienate her from seeking help.

questions.questionText:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother because he has become unmanageable at his day-care center. At 
previous  visits  he  exhibited  behavior  problems  to  which  his  mother  did  not  set  limits.  He  constantly  interrupted  
situations, seeking his mother's attention. She now reports that during the past few months his fighting, refusal to obey 
the day-care workers, and violations of "time out" have become much worse. He began day care at 6 weeks of age so 
that his mother could return to work. His father works as a house painter and has alcoholism. The boy has a 6-month-
old sister who also attends the same day-care center. Records show his height and weight are at the 5th percentile, and 
his growth velocity is normal. There were no complications during the pregnancy with this child and he has not had 
any  significant  medical  problems.  Physical  examination  today  discloses  no  abnormalities.  Which  of  the  following  is  
the most likely cause for this child's worsening behavior?

choices.A: Aggressiveness to compensate for a poor self-image caused by short stature
choices.C: A reaction to his father's drinking
choices.D: Reduction in his mother's attention because of his new sibling
choices.E: A toxic reaction to organic fumes from his father's clothes and work materials

explanations.A: Short stature at the 5th percentile with normal growth velocity is not pathological and is unlikely to cause significant psychological disturbance in a 4-year-old who does not yet have awareness of comparative height. There is no evidence that the child's behavior is driven by poor self-image related to size.
explanations.B: ADHD is a consideration for disruptive behavior, but the worsening of symptoms over a defined recent period (past few months) with an identifiable psychosocial trigger argues against a primary neurodevelopmental diagnosis at this visit. ADHD is a diagnosis of exclusion that requires ruling out situational causes first.
explanations.C: Paternal alcoholism is a chronic stressor that has been present throughout the child's life, so it cannot explain why behavior has specifically worsened over the past few months. A change in the child's environment is a more likely explanation for the acute deterioration.
explanations.D: The birth of a sibling 6 months ago is the most likely cause of the worsening behavior — sibling rivalry and competition for maternal attention are well-established triggers for regression and behavioral escalation in young children. The timeline correlates directly with the onset of worsened fighting and defiance at day care.
explanations.E: Toxic exposure to organic solvents from a parent's work is an uncommon and speculative cause of behavioral problems, with no supporting evidence in this history. Solvent neurotoxicity typically presents with cognitive or neurological signs rather than pure oppositional behavior.

questions.questionText:
A 13-year-old girl is brought to the office for a health maintenance visit. She was diagnosed with Turner syndrome in 
infancy  during  a  work-up  for  coarctation  of  the  aorta.  During  today's  visit,  her  mother  reports  that  the  girl  has  been  
talking  about  babies.  You  have  been  the  patient's  physician  for  the  past  6  years  and  know  she  is  prepubescent.  You  
counsel the girl that if she wishes to have a family she will need to do which of the following?

choices.B: Have amniocentesis if she gets pregnant

explanations.A: Adoption is the correct answer because Turner syndrome (45,X) causes primary ovarian failure (streak gonads) and virtually universal infertility — affected individuals cannot produce viable oocytes. Without functional gonads, pregnancy via her own eggs is not possible, making adoption the counseling point for having a family.
explanations.B: Amniocentesis screens for chromosomal abnormalities during pregnancy, but since this patient cannot conceive with her own eggs due to ovarian failure, amniocentesis during a spontaneous pregnancy is not applicable. Even with donor-egg IVF, the question asks about the most direct counseling for family planning.
explanations.C: There is no surgical intervention that can restore ovarian function or fertility in Turner syndrome. While some surgical procedures may be needed for associated conditions (e.g., aortic coarctation repair), surgery does not address the inability to conceive.
explanations.D: Genetic counseling is appropriate for many reproductive questions but is not the primary counseling point here — Turner syndrome is well-characterized, and the key issue is infertility due to ovarian failure, not recurrence risk in offspring. Adoption does not require genetic counseling as a prerequisite.
explanations.E: Hormone treatment (estrogen/progesterone replacement) is necessary to induce puberty and maintain bone density in Turner syndrome, but it does not restore fertility. Hormone therapy addresses secondary sex characteristics and cardiovascular protection, not oocyte production.

questions.questionText:
You are asked to evaluate a 78-year-old German American woman who is admitted to the hospital for replacement of 
her left knee joint due to degenerative joint disease. She is a retired seamstress. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus, a long 
history  of  hypertension,  and  chronic  renal  failure  presumed  secondary  to  diabetes  mellitus  and  hypertension.  
Reversible  causes  of  renal  failure  have  been  excluded.  She  underwent  a  tonsillectomy  at  age  9  years  and  a  
laparoscopic  cholecystectomy  at  age  68  years.  Serum  creatinine  concentration  on  admission  was  6.0  mg/dL.  Her  
current  therapy  includes  a  low-sodium,  low-protein  American  Diabetes  Association  (ADA)  diet,  enalapril,  and  
acetaminophen.  She  and  her  husband  live  on  a  farm  90  miles  from  the  nearest  dialysis  facility.  In  considering  long-
term treatment options for this patient, which of the following is the most appropriate factor to consider?

choices.B: Her history of an abdominal operation
choices.E: Her willingness to move to the city

explanations.A: Medicare eligibility for end-stage renal disease (ESRD) is essentially universal for U.S. citizens regardless of age, so it is not a discriminating factor in choosing a dialysis modality. Financing should not drive the selection of the most appropriate clinical treatment.
explanations.B: Her prior laparoscopic cholecystectomy could create intraperitoneal adhesions that might complicate peritoneal dialysis catheter placement, but this is a secondary technical consideration rather than the primary planning factor. It would be evaluated if peritoneal dialysis were being seriously considered.
explanations.C: Her arthritis affects her mobility and daily function but is not the most important factor in selecting a long-term renal replacement modality. Joint disease alone does not determine dialysis suitability.
explanations.D: Home dialysis — either home hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis — is the most appropriate consideration because she lives 90 miles from the nearest dialysis center, making in-center hemodialysis three times per week logistically impractical and burdensome. Assessing her suitability for a home-based modality directly addresses her geographic barrier.
explanations.E: Requiring her to move to the city to access dialysis ignores patient-centered care principles and assumes relocation is acceptable. The goal is to match the treatment to the patient's life circumstances, not to require the patient to restructure her life entirely around treatment logistics.

questions.questionText:
A 60-year-old man is admitted to the hospital for management of acute pancreatitis. Results of laboratory studies are shown: 
Serum                        Blood
  Amylase       1000 U/L       Hematocrit    42%
  Calcium       8.4 mg/dL      WBC           14,000/mm³
  Urea nitrogen 5 mg/dL
Results of serum liver chemistry profile are within the reference ranges. After 48 hours of fluid therapy and observation, a poor prognosis would be indicated by which of the following laboratory studies?

choices.A: Serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT) concentration of 106 U/L

explanations.A: Elevated ALT at 106 U/L suggests mild hepatocellular injury, which can occur in gallstone pancreatitis, but it is not one of Ranson's criteria for poor prognosis at 48 hours. Liver enzyme elevation alone does not independently predict severity or mortality in acute pancreatitis.
explanations.B: Serum amylase levels do not correlate with severity or prognosis in acute pancreatitis — very high amylase can be seen in mild disease and normal amylase in severe disease. Amylase is useful for diagnosis, not prognosis.
explanations.C: Elevated bilirubin at 48 hours is not a standard Ranson's prognostic criterion; it may suggest biliary obstruction but does not independently predict pancreatic necrosis or systemic complications. It is part of the initial assessment, not the 48-hour deterioration markers.
explanations.D: Serum calcium of 6.6 mg/dL (hypocalcemia) at 48 hours is a classic Ranson's criterion indicating poor prognosis — calcium is sequestered in areas of fat saponification caused by lipase-mediated necrosis, reflecting extensive pancreatic and peripancreatic tissue destruction. This finding carries significant mortality risk.
explanations.E: Blood glucose greater than 200 mg/dL is actually a Ranson's criterion assessed on admission (not at 48 hours) in non-diabetic patients. At 48 hours, the relevant criteria include calcium drop, hematocrit fall, BUN rise, PO2 decline, base deficit, and fluid sequestration — not a glucose of 200 in a patient who may have baseline diabetes.

questions.questionText:
A 68-year-old white man comes to the office because of progressively worsening right groin pain for the past month. 
You  last  saw  the  patient  3  months  ago  for  preoperative  assessment  for  a  total  right  hip  replacement.  For  the  past  
5  years  he  has  been  treated  with  inhaled  bronchodilators  for  emphysema  resulting  from  an  extensive  history  of  
smoking cigarettes. The patient is widowed and has lived alone in a mobile home since his wife died 4 years ago. Vital 
signs  today  are  temperature  36.8°C  (98.2°F),  pulse  66/min,  respirations  18/min,  and  blood  pressure  110/82  mm  Hg.  
Physical examination shows an unkempt man who appears much older than his stated age. He has evidence of alcohol 
on  his  breath;  dentition  is  poor  with  several  broken,  loose  teeth,  and  gingival  pyorrhea  is  present.  Lung  sounds  are  
distant  and  air  entry  is  poor.  Heart  and  abdomen  are  normal.  There  is  no  hernia  present  in  the  groin.  With  the  
exception  of  testicular  atrophy,  the  patient's  genitalia  are  normal.  Which  of  the  following  is  the  most  likely  initial  
working diagnosis?

explanations.A: Iliac venous thrombosis would present with leg swelling, edema, and possibly superficial venous dilation rather than isolated groin pain in a patient who recently had hip surgery. The presentation and risk factors here point more strongly to a prosthetic complication.
explanations.B: Infected hip prosthesis is the correct working diagnosis — the patient had a total right hip replacement 3 months ago and now presents with right groin pain, alcohol use, poor dentition with gingival pyorrhea, and signs of neglected self-care. Bacteremia from oral sources (periodontal disease) is a well-recognized mechanism of hematogenous seeding of joint prostheses.
explanations.C: Occult appendicitis would be unusual in this location and clinical context; appendicitis typically presents with periumbilical pain migrating to the right iliac fossa, fever, and leukocytosis, none of which are prominent here. The prior prosthetic surgery is a more compelling explanatory factor.
explanations.D: Occult hernia is excluded by physical examination, which specifically notes no hernia present in the groin. A physical finding cannot be the answer when the exam has already ruled it out.
explanations.E: Somatoform disorder is a diagnosis of exclusion and should not be entertained when a clear organic cause — prosthetic joint infection — has not yet been fully evaluated. The patient has objective risk factors (recent joint replacement, bacteremic source) that demand a structural diagnosis first.

questions.questionText:
A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his father because of lethargy. On physical examination, the boy is slightly lethargic and has deep respirations, which are 32/min. The father, who is a single parent, says, "He is always thirsty and he pees a lot." Results of laboratory studies are shown: 
Serum
  Glucose    850 mg/dL
  Na⁺       132 mEq/L
  K⁺        4.1 mEq/L
  Cl⁻        92 mEq/L
  HCO₃⁻     6 mEq/L
After admitting the boy to the hospital, which of the following is the most appropriate therapy?

choices.A: Administer normal saline and add potassium once urinary output is adequate
choices.B: Correct the acidosis with oral bicarbonate solution
choices.C: Correct the dehydration with hypotonic saline solution
choices.D: Give phenobarbital to prevent hyponatremic seizures
choices.E: Institute    intermediate-acting    insulin to correct hyperglycemia

explanations.A: Normal saline is the correct initial fluid for DKA/HHS resuscitation because it corrects hypovolemia and hyperosmolality without worsening hyponatremia; potassium is added only after urinary output is confirmed to be adequate, preventing dangerous hyperkalemia in a patient who may have impaired renal excretion. This is the standard of care for pediatric diabetic emergencies.
explanations.B: Oral bicarbonate is inappropriate in a critically ill child with obtundation and Kussmaul respirations — oral administration is unsafe due to aspiration risk and inadequate for the degree of acidosis (HCO3 of 6 mEq/L). IV bicarbonate use in DKA is itself controversial and generally reserved for severe acidosis (pH < 6.9) under careful monitoring.
explanations.C: Hypotonic saline should not be used as initial resuscitation fluid in diabetic emergencies because it can cause rapid osmolality shifts leading to cerebral edema, a feared complication in pediatric DKA. Normal saline provides safer initial volume repletion.
explanations.D: Phenobarbital to prevent hyponatremic seizures is not indicated — the sodium is mildly low (132 mEq/L) and will correct with appropriate fluid therapy. Prophylactic anticonvulsants are not part of DKA management, and seizure prevention in this context is addressed by controlled osmolality correction.
explanations.E: Intermediate-acting insulin (e.g., NPH) is not appropriate for acute DKA management, which requires short-acting or regular insulin given intravenously to allow precise titration. Intermediate-acting formulations have delayed onset and unpredictable absorption in a hemodynamically unstable patient.

questions.questionText:
A 74-year-old white man with dementia is transferred to the emergency department from a nursing facility because of 
necrosis  of  the  distal  phalanx  of  the  second  toe  on  his  right  foot.  He  denies  localizing  pain.  He  has  a  history  of  
hypertension,  coronary  artery  disease,  congestive  heart  failure,  and  mild  renal  insufficiency.  Vital  signs  are  
temperature  37.0°C  (98.6°F),  pulse  92/min,  respirations  16/min,  and  blood  pressure  160/80  mm  Hg.  On  physical  
examination  he  is  thin  and  is  in  no  distress.  Chest  has  bilateral  crackles  at  both  lung  bases.  Cardiac  and  abdominal  
examination discloses no abnormalities. The extremities are thin and the distal phalanx of the second right toe is black. 
Mild erythema is present on the adjacent proximal skin but no purulence is noted. Bilateral carotid and femoral artery 
bruits  are  noted.  Radial  pulses  are  full.  A  pulsatile  mass  measuring  approximately  2  cm  is  palpable  in  the  right  
popliteal fossa. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 3+ bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely 
cause of this patient's gangrene?

explanations.A: Arterial embolus is the correct answer — the patient has preserved distal pulses (3+ dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial bilaterally), which argues against diffuse obliterative disease, and a pulsatile popliteal mass (likely popliteal artery aneurysm) that is a well-known source of distal thromboembolism. Small emboli from an aneurysm selectively occlude digital vessels, producing isolated toe gangrene with intact proximal circulation.
explanations.B: A hypercoagulable state is possible but less likely than the structural embolic source already identified on exam — the popliteal aneurysm provides a compelling mechanical explanation for the digital ischemia. Thrombophilia workup may follow, but it is not the most likely primary mechanism.
explanations.C: Obliterative angiopathy (e.g., atherosclerotic peripheral arterial disease) typically presents with absent or diminished distal pulses, claudication, and diffuse limb ischemia — not isolated toe gangrene with robust 3+ pedal pulses bilaterally. The pulse exam effectively rules this out.
explanations.D: Polyarteritis nodosa is a medium-vessel vasculitis that could cause digital ischemia but would typically present with systemic features such as weight loss, hypertension, mononeuritis multiplex, and elevated inflammatory markers. There is no suggestion of systemic vasculitis in this vignette.
explanations.E: Raynaud syndrome causes episodic digital vasospasm triggered by cold or stress, presenting with color changes (white-blue-red sequence) rather than fixed necrosis of a single digit. It does not explain irreversible gangrene and is not associated with popliteal aneurysm.

questions.questionText:
A  76-year-old  German  American  man  comes  to  the  office  because  of  early  awakening  at  night.  He  has  no  difficulty  
falling  asleep  but  routinely  wakes  up  between  2:00  and  3:00  
AM.  The  patient  is  a  retired  postal  worker,  and  he  has  
always been physically active. He has diabetes mellitus controlled by diet. He is not obese. The patient drinks one cup 
of coffee in the morning with breakfast and usually walks for exercise in the morning. Before retiring at night he has 
one  alcoholic  drink.  He  has  no  history  of  depression,  nightmares,  or  snoring  and  he  takes  no  over-the-counter  
medications. His wife of 45 years is also in good health. Vital signs are temperature 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse 96/min and 
regular, respirations 18/min, and blood pressure 135/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a well-nourished, well-
developed man. Examination of the head and neck is normal; there are no bruits or jugular venous distention. Chest is 
clear, heart is normal with S

 and S

. Abdomen is soft and nontender with active bowel sounds and no organomegaly. 
Rectal examination is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate management of this patient's insomnia?

choices.A: Advise the patient to discontinue his bedtime drink of alcohol
choices.B: Advise the patient to read and snack in bed to relax
choices.C: Prescribe a vigorous pre-bedtime exercise regimen

explanations.A: Advising discontinuation of the bedtime alcoholic drink is correct — alcohol disrupts sleep architecture by suppressing REM sleep and causing rebound arousal in the second half of the night as alcohol is metabolized, which explains early morning awakening (2–3 AM). Eliminating this behavior is the most targeted, non-pharmacological intervention.
explanations.B: Reading and snacking in bed reinforces the association between the bed and wakefulness, which is counterproductive in sleep hygiene. Good sleep hygiene requires using the bed only for sleep and sex to strengthen the conditioned sleep response.
explanations.C: Vigorous pre-bedtime exercise raises core body temperature and increases sympathetic tone, which delays sleep onset and can fragment sleep. Exercise is beneficial for insomnia but should occur earlier in the day, not immediately before bed.
explanations.D: Sertraline (an SSRI) is indicated for insomnia associated with depression, which this patient does not have. Prescribing an antidepressant without a psychiatric indication is inappropriate and exposes the patient to unnecessary side effects.
explanations.E: Triazolam is a short-acting benzodiazepine that could temporarily improve sleep but does not address the underlying behavioral cause (bedtime alcohol use) and carries risks of dependence, rebound insomnia, and cognitive effects in older adults. Behavioral intervention should always precede pharmacotherapy.

questions.setVignette:
A  1-month-old  white  boy  whom  you  saw  at  birth  is  brought  to  the  office  for  a  routine  well-child  visit.  His  birth  history  is  
unremarkable except for mild jaundice that subsided within a few  days. His  birth weight  was 2718 g (6 lb; 10th percentile), 
and length was 50 cm (19.5 in; 30th percentile). His mother stopped breast-feeding when he was 6 days old. At today's visit, 
the    infant    is    alert    and    active,    and    he    cries    until    fed.    He    weighs    3.2 kg (7 lb; 5th percentile),    and    is 
54.6 cm (21.5 in; 25th percentile)  long.  His  mother  tells  you  that,  even  when  cuddled,  the  infant  cries  continually  until  fed.
