He always eats slowly, falling asleep before he finishes his formula. You find that formula flows adequately from the bottle 
nipple,  and  you  detect  no  abnormalities  on  physical  examination  of  the  boy  to  explain  his  slow  feeding.  Urination  and  
stooling  patterns  are  normal.  Serum  urea  nitrogen  concentration  is  10  mg/dL  and  serum  thyroxine  (T

)  concentration  is  
10 μg/dL.
questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.A: Advise his mother to resume breast-feeding
choices.B: Hospitalize the infant for parenteral hyperalimentation
choices.E: Schedule him for placement of a feeding gastrostomy tube

explanations.A: Resuming breastfeeding alone is insufficient if the infant has demonstrated failure to thrive — the context implies inadequate weight gain, and breast milk provides only approximately 20 calories/oz, which may be inadequate for catch-up growth. The clinical situation calls for a calorie-dense formula.
explanations.B: Hospitalization for parenteral hyperalimentation (TPN) is an extreme intervention reserved for infants who cannot tolerate enteral feeding or have severe malnutrition. This infant's failure to thrive does not yet meet criteria for invasive nutritional support.
explanations.C: Prescribing hypercaloric formula (24 cal/oz) is the correct next step — the infant has failure to thrive (weight below birth weight percentile with inadequate gain), and increasing caloric density is the standard initial intervention to promote catch-up growth in the outpatient setting. This provides approximately 20% more calories per volume without significantly increasing feeding burden.
explanations.D: Liquid multivitamins address micronutrient deficiency, not caloric insufficiency, which is the primary issue in failure to thrive. Vitamins alone will not restore adequate weight gain.
explanations.E: Gastrostomy tube placement is a surgical intervention reserved for infants with structural feeding disorders, neurological impairment, or failed conservative nutritional management. It is not appropriate as a first-line step in an infant who has not yet tried caloric augmentation.

questions.questionText:
At a follow-up visit 1 week later, which of the following factors would most likely indicate satisfactory progress in the 
treatment of the infant's problem?

choices.A: The infant does not cry before feeding
choices.B: The infant finishes his formula at each feeding
choices.C: The infant no longer falls asleep while feeding
choices.D: The infant's weight increases by at least 0.2 kg (0.4 lb)
choices.E: Serum urea nitrogen concentration decreases to 6 mg/dL

explanations.A: Whether the infant cries before feeding reflects hunger cues but does not measure the adequacy of nutritional recovery — an infant can be quiet yet still gaining weight poorly. Behavioral cues are unreliable markers of treatment success compared to objective weight gain.
explanations.B: Finishing formula at each feeding suggests adequate appetite but does not confirm sufficient caloric intake for catch-up growth — the infant may need more volume or frequency, not just completion of each bottle. Volume consumed is not equivalent to weight gain.
explanations.C: An infant falling asleep while feeding is a sign of low energy and fatigue due to caloric deficiency; while no longer falling asleep is an improvement, it is a symptom rather than a direct measure of nutritional adequacy. Weight gain remains the primary outcome measure.
explanations.D: A weight gain of at least 0.2 kg (200 g) in one week is the correct indicator of satisfactory progress — this corresponds to approximately 30 g/day, which exceeds the minimum expected gain for catch-up growth in a failing-to-thrive infant. Objective weight gain is the standard metric for assessing nutritional intervention success.
explanations.E: A decrease in serum urea nitrogen to 6 mg/dL reflects protein catabolism status but is not the standard clinical endpoint for monitoring catch-up growth in an infant with failure to thrive. Weight gain is the primary and most reliable clinical measure.

questions.questionText:
A 62-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of obtundation. On physical examination, she has hypotension and tachycardia. Respirations are 24/min. She has cherry-red maculae on funduscopic examination. Results of initial laboratory studies are shown: 
Serum                          Urine
  Urea nitrogen  37 mg/dL        Color              Clear
  Na⁺           139 mEq/L        Specific gravity   1.010
  K⁺            6.1 mEq/L        Glucose            Negative
  Cl⁻           100 mEq/L        Proteins, total    2+
  HCO₃⁻         10 mEq/L         Ketones            Trace
  Glucose        121 mg/dL        WBC                5-10/hpf
  Osmolality     357 mOsmol/kg H₂O  RBC              3-5/hpf
Arterial blood gas on room air   Crystals           None
  Po₂            75 mm Hg         Casts              Rare epithelial cell casts
  Pco₂           26 mm Hg
  pH             7.09
  HCO₃⁻          9 mEq/L
Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these data?

explanations.A: Alcoholic ketoacidosis typically presents with an elevated anion gap and elevated osmolal gap but would show positive serum ketones and a history of alcohol use; the serum osmolality here is markedly elevated (357 mOsm/kg) with a large osmolal gap but no ketones, and cherry-red maculae are not a feature of alcoholic ketoacidosis.
explanations.B: Diabetic ketoacidosis would present with markedly elevated glucose, positive urine ketones, and a history of diabetes; this patient's glucose is only 121 mg/dL, making DKA unlikely. Cherry-red macular lesions are not a feature of DKA.
explanations.C: Isopropyl alcohol intoxication causes an elevated osmolal gap and ketonemia (isopropanol is metabolized to acetone) but does NOT cause a metabolic acidosis — there is no increase in the anion gap because acetone is not an acid. This patient has a severe anion gap acidosis (pH 7.09, HCO3 9), excluding isopropyl alcohol.
explanations.D: Methanol intoxication is correct — it produces a severe anion gap metabolic acidosis (formic acid accumulates), a markedly elevated osmolal gap (from the parent compound), and the pathognomonic finding of cherry-red maculae (optic disc hyperemia/retinal toxicity from formate). The low pH, high osmolality, absence of ketones, and visual findings all fit methanol poisoning.
explanations.E: Salicylate intoxication causes a mixed respiratory alkalosis and anion gap metabolic acidosis, often with tinnitus and hyperpyrexia; cherry-red maculae are not a feature and the osmolal gap is not elevated. The clinical picture here does not fit salicylate toxicity.

questions.questionText:
A  15-year-old  African  American  girl  comes  to  the  emergency  department  because,  she  says,  "something  has  been  
sticking out of my bottom since I had a bowel movement this morning." She has not had previous episodes, although 
for  more  than  1  year  she  has  had  occasional  difficulty  passing  stools.  She  is  not  in  pain  but  is  afraid  to  move  her  
bowels for fear that the problem will worsen. She tells you that she moved away from home more than a year ago and 
that  her  parents  contribute  nothing  to  her  support.  She  has  a  6-month-old  child  and  lives  with  a  28-year-old  female  
cousin. She has never been married and does not work or attend school. She has no other symptoms. In order to follow 
the correct procedure for treating a minor, which of the following is the most appropriate step prior to evaluating this 
patient's rectal problem?

choices.A: Accept the girl's consent as sufficient
choices.B: Obtain a court order permitting evaluation
choices.C: Obtain the written consent of at least two licensed physicians
choices.D: Obtain written consent from at least one of her parents
choices.E: Obtain written consent from her 28-year-old cousin

explanations.A: Accepting the girl's consent as sufficient is correct — she qualifies as an emancipated minor because she has left home, is financially independent (receives no parental support), and has a child of her own. Emancipated minors have full legal authority to consent to their own medical care without parental or court involvement.
explanations.B: A court order is required for treatment of an unemancipated minor when parents are unavailable and the situation is non-emergent, but this patient is legally emancipated and does not require court authorization. Seeking a court order would cause unnecessary delay.
explanations.C: The requirement for two licensed physician signatures applies to specific legal determinations (e.g., involuntary psychiatric commitment) and is not a requirement for obtaining consent for medical evaluation. This is not the applicable legal standard here.
explanations.D: Parental consent is required for non-emancipated minors; however, this patient is an emancipated minor by virtue of her independent living status and parenthood. Requiring parental consent in this case is legally unnecessary and may violate her privacy rights.
explanations.E: An adult cousin, even one with whom the minor lives, has no legal authority to provide medical consent on her behalf. Consent authority rests with the minor herself (if emancipated) or her legal guardian (if not emancipated), not with relatives.

questions.questionText:
A  35-year-old  white  man  with  spina  bifida  is  admitted  to  the  hospital  for  a  urologic  procedure.  He  has  been  
functionally independent in activities of daily living and is employed doing inventory control in a local sporting goods 
store.  He  has  maintained  continence  through  periodic  self-catheterization.  The  patient  is  paraplegic,  has  recurrent  
calcium oxalate kidney stones, and recent onset of incontinence secondary to detrusor and bladder neck dysfunction. 
Vital signs are normal. Physical examination shows a well-developed, well-nourished man in no acute distress. Aside 
from  paraplegia,  lower  extremity  muscle  atrophy,  and  lower  abdominal  surgical  scars,  the  physical  examination  
discloses  no  abnormalities.  He  had  an  episode  of  anaphylaxis  secondary  to  latex  allergy  during  a  previous  operation  
for  functional  expansion  of  his  bladder  through  a  bowel  anastomosis.  Which  of  the  following  is  most  important  to  
consider in the care of this patient?

choices.A: Administration of injectable medications with disposable syringes
choices.B: Preparation of food by outside contractors
choices.C: Type of cleaning agents used to sterilize bed linens
choices.E: Use of topical moisturizing agents for skin care

explanations.A: Disposable syringes are made of plastic and do not contain latex; the latex allergy risk comes from rubber-stoppered vials and rubber gloves, not the syringes themselves. This intervention does not address the primary allergy risk.
explanations.B: Food preparation by outside contractors is irrelevant to latex allergy management in a hospital setting — latex allergy does not involve food sources unless the patient also has latex-fruit syndrome, which is not indicated here. This is not a meaningful safety measure.
explanations.C: Cleaning agents used to sterilize bed linens are not a source of latex exposure; latex allergy is triggered by proteins in natural rubber latex products. This is not a clinically relevant consideration for this patient.
explanations.D: Rubber urethral catheters contain natural latex and represent the highest-risk latex exposure for this patient, given his history of anaphylaxis and need for urologic procedures including catheterization. All latex-containing devices — especially catheters, gloves, and surgical drains — must be replaced with latex-free alternatives.
explanations.E: Topical moisturizing agents for skin care do not contain latex and are not a source of latex exposure. While skin integrity is important in paraplegic patients, this does not address the life-threatening allergy risk.

questions.questionText:
A  14-year-old  boy  is  brought  to  the  walk-in  clinic  by  his  father  late  on  a  Saturday  afternoon  because  his  left  ear  is  
swollen and painful. The ear has been black and blue since he injured it in a wrestling match 3 days ago. Symptoms 
have increased significantly following a repeat injury during a match 3 hours ago. Vital signs are normal. The left ear 
is markedly swollen and tender to palpation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.A: Reassure him and start aspirin therapy
choices.B: Reassure him and start codeine therapy
choices.C: Recommend that he apply cold packs to the ear for the next 12 hours
choices.D: Recommend that he apply hot packs to the ear for the next 12 hours
choices.E: Refer him to a surgeon for immediate drainage of the lesion

explanations.A: Aspirin therapy alone provides analgesia but does not address the underlying auricular hematoma, which requires drainage to prevent avascular necrosis of the cartilage. Without evacuation, the hematoma will organize, leading to permanent cartilage destruction ('cauliflower ear').
explanations.B: Codeine therapy manages pain but does not treat the hematoma itself — opioid analgesia without drainage allows the clot to organize and perichondrial blood supply to be disrupted, resulting in cartilaginous fibrosis. Pain management is adjunctive, not definitive treatment.
explanations.C: Cold packs may reduce swelling in the acute phase of minor contusions but are not appropriate for an established, worsening auricular hematoma over 3 days. Cold therapy does not evacuate the hematoma and will not prevent cartilage necrosis.
explanations.D: Hot packs are contraindicated for hematomas — heat increases vasodilation and fluid accumulation, potentially expanding the hematoma and worsening pressure on the overlying cartilage. This is the opposite of appropriate management.
explanations.E: Immediate surgical drainage is correct — an auricular hematoma that has been present for 3 days and is worsening requires urgent incision and drainage followed by a compressive bolster dressing to prevent re-accumulation. Delayed drainage risks permanent cartilage necrosis and deformity.

questions.questionText:
A 25-year-old woman who is 19 weeks pregnant comes to the office for a prenatal examination. Her father had classic 
hemophilia. A karyotype obtained from an amniotic fluid sample of the patient shows that the fetus is XY. Which of 
the following should you tell the patient regarding her infant?

choices.A: The infant will neither have hemophilia nor be a carrier
choices.B: The infant has a 50% risk for hemophilia
choices.C: The infant has a 50% risk for being a carrier
choices.D: The infant has a 75% risk for hemophilia
choices.E: The infant has a 75% risk for being a carrier

explanations.A: It is incorrect that the infant will have no risk — classic hemophilia A is X-linked recessive, and the patient's father has hemophilia, meaning she is an obligate carrier (she received her father's affected X chromosome). A carrier mother has a 50% chance of passing the affected X to a son.
explanations.B: A 50% risk for hemophilia is correct — the mother is an obligate carrier of hemophilia A (her father was affected, so she received his X^h). The fetus is XY (male), and males get their X chromosome from their mother: there is a 50% chance the son inherits the X^h allele and will have hemophilia.
explanations.C: Males cannot be carriers of X-linked recessive conditions — they are either affected (X^h Y) or unaffected (X Y). The concept of a male carrier does not apply; carrier status is a female phenomenon for X-linked recessive disorders.
explanations.D: A 75% risk would apply to a scenario where both parents are carriers or one parent is affected and one is a carrier — this is not the case here. The mother is a carrier (not affected) and the father's genotype is not specified as contributing additional risk.
explanations.E: Males cannot be carriers of X-linked recessive disorders, so a 75% carrier risk is biologically impossible for a male fetus. Only females can carry one affected allele while being phenotypically unaffected.

questions.questionText:
An  18-month-old  child  is  brought  to  the  emergency  department  by  his  parents  because  of  fever  and  irritability.  The  
child was well until 4 days ago when he developed rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and diarrhea. One week ago, he was 
seen in the office by your partner and received routine vaccinations at that time. His mother says he felt warm to touch 
2 days ago, but his temperature was not taken. She has been giving him acetaminophen every 6 hours during the past 
2  days.  Since  this  morning,  the  child  has  been  eating  poorly.  There  is  a  4-year-old  sibling  at  home  who  is  healthy.  
Vital signs are temperature 38.2°C (100.8°F), pulse 100/min, respirations 25/min, and blood pressure 100/70 mm Hg. 
On physical examination, the child is irritable. He is uncooperative during the examination, and his neck is stiff and 
painful when flexed. He is whining that he wants to go home. Extremities are cool, with normal capillary refill time, 
and there is no rash. These findings are most consistent with which of the following?

explanations.A: Acetaminophen toxicity presents with hepatotoxicity (elevated liver enzymes, jaundice) in the days following overdose, not with meningismus. The dose described (every 6 hours for 2 days) is within therapeutic range and would not explain neck stiffness.
explanations.B: Aseptic (viral) meningitis is the most consistent diagnosis — the child has fever, irritability, nuchal rigidity (neck stiff and painful when flexed), and a preceding viral prodrome (rhinorrhea, diarrhea). The absence of rash, normal capillary refill, and relatively mild fever suggest viral rather than bacterial etiology, and the clinical syndrome fits aseptic meningitis.
explanations.C: Gastroenteritis with mild dehydration would not explain nuchal rigidity, which is a meningeal sign requiring CNS evaluation. Dehydration alone does not cause neck stiffness.
explanations.D: Reye syndrome presents with encephalopathy and hepatic dysfunction following viral illness treated with aspirin; this child received acetaminophen, not aspirin, making Reye syndrome very unlikely. Additionally, Reye syndrome does not cause nuchal rigidity.
explanations.E: Vaccine reactions such as fever and irritability can occur within 1–3 days of vaccination, but the child was vaccinated one week ago — beyond the typical reaction window. More importantly, vaccine reactions do not cause nuchal rigidity, which mandates consideration of meningitis.

questions.questionText:
A 22-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her parents because of muscular weakness, muscle 
twitches, and palpitations. She is extremely thin and somewhat cachectic. Her parents report that she has a history of 
self-induced vomiting and overuse of laxatives and thiazide diuretics. Routine blood studies are obtained. Which of the 
following findings is most likely to explain these signs and symptoms?

explanations.A: Decreased hematocrit and hemoglobin (anemia) cause fatigue, pallor, and dyspnea but do not cause muscle twitches (fasciculations) or palpitations with cardiac dysrhythmia. Anemia may be present in this patient but does not explain the neuromuscular and cardiac symptoms.
explanations.B: Hypoglycemia causes diaphoresis, tremor, altered mental status, and seizures, but not muscle twitches or cardiac palpitations as described. While nutritional deficiency is possible in this patient, hypoglycemia is not the best explanation for the combination of findings.
explanations.C: Decreased serum potassium (hypokalemia) is the correct answer — vomiting, laxative abuse, and thiazide diuretic use all cause renal and gastrointestinal potassium wasting. Hypokalemia causes skeletal muscle weakness, fasciculations, and cardiac arrhythmias (palpitations) by altering resting membrane potential.
explanations.D: Increased serum calcium (hypercalcemia) causes constipation, polyuria, bone pain, and confusion (stones, bones, groans, psychiatric moans) — not muscle twitches. Hypocalcemia causes tetany and twitching, not hypercalcemia.
explanations.E: Increased serum sodium (hypernatremia) causes neurological symptoms such as confusion, lethargy, and seizures in severe cases, but does not explain muscle twitches and palpitations in this clinical context. The purging behaviors described cause electrolyte depletion, not sodium accumulation.

questions.questionText:
A  50-year-old  woman  comes  to  the  office  for  the  first  time  because  of  recurrent  abdominal  pain.  Review  of  her  
extensive  medical  chart,  which  she  has  brought  with  her,  discloses  that  she  has  a  long  history  of  varying  physical  
complaints.  Definitive  causes  for  these  complaints  have  not  been  found  despite  extensive  diagnostic  studies,  
consultations   with   many   physicians,   and   several   surgical   explorations.   She   gives   dramatic   and   exaggerated   
descriptions of her present and past symptoms, and she makes conflicting statements about her history. She has been 
hospitalized at least 23 times since age 18 years. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanations.A: Borderline personality disorder involves unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, self-harm, and affective instability — it does not characterize a pattern of medically unexplained physical symptoms with extensive hospitalizations as the primary presentation. While personality disorders can co-exist, the dominant syndrome here is somatic.
explanations.B: Conversion disorder involves neurological symptoms (e.g., paralysis, blindness, seizures) that cannot be explained by a neurological lesion, and it is typically a single, acute episode rather than a lifelong pattern of multiple organ system complaints. The chronic, polysymptomatic course here distinguishes it from conversion disorder.
explanations.C: Histrionic personality disorder is characterized by attention-seeking, emotionality, and theatrical behavior; while the patient shows dramatic symptom descriptions, histrionic PD alone does not explain 23 hospitalizations and multiple unexplained multisystem complaints across decades. The primary diagnosis driving the medical utilization is somatic.
explanations.D: An occult medical disorder is always a diagnostic consideration, but the pattern of symptoms across multiple organ systems, extensive negative workup, multiple surgical explorations, and decades of unexplained complaints makes an undiagnosed organic disease extremely unlikely. This answer reflects anchoring bias.
explanations.E: Somatization disorder (DSM-IV; now Somatic Symptom Disorder in DSM-5) is the correct diagnosis — it requires a history of multiple medically unexplained physical symptoms beginning before age 30, involving multiple organ systems, with significant medical utilization. This patient's lifelong pattern of polysymptomatic complaints, dramatic presentation, and extensive negative workup is the textbook presentation.

questions.questionText:
A  27-year-old  woman  comes  to  the  office  for  her  annual  physical  examination  and  says,  "Two  weeks  ago  I  noticed  
some small bumps on the outside of my vagina. They don't hurt, but they do itch a little." She has never been pregnant 
and she takes an oral contraceptive. Physical examination shows several small, moist warts on the labia minora. VDRL 
test  is  negative.  Pap  smear  shows  moderate  high-grade  squamous  intraepithelial  lesions  (HGSIL;  CIN2).  After  
removal of the vaginal warts, which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

explanations.A: Colposcopy is the correct next step — the Pap smear showing high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (HSIL/CIN2) requires direct visual examination of the cervix with magnification and directed biopsy to confirm the grade of dysplasia and guide definitive treatment. Colposcopy is the standard of care following HSIL on cytology.
explanations.B: Cone biopsy (LEEP or cold knife) is a therapeutic and diagnostic procedure for CIN2-3, but it should follow colposcopy to confirm the diagnosis and map the extent of the lesion — performing cone biopsy without prior colposcopy skips a necessary diagnostic step. Colposcopy identifies the transformation zone and guides the extent of excision.
explanations.C: Endometrial biopsy evaluates the uterine cavity for endometrial pathology and is indicated for abnormal uterine bleeding or postmenopausal bleeding — it has no role in the evaluation of cervical dysplasia or HPV-related condylomata. This is the wrong anatomic target.
explanations.D: Hysterectomy is a definitive treatment for cervical dysplasia that has failed conservative management or in cases of invasive cancer, but it is not appropriate without first confirming the diagnosis and stage with colposcopy and biopsy. Proceeding directly to hysterectomy for a Pap smear result is excessive and premature.
explanations.E: Repeat Pap smear in 6 months is an acceptable approach for LSIL (CIN1) or atypical squamous cells (ASC-US), but HSIL (CIN2) on cytology requires immediate colposcopic evaluation rather than watchful waiting. Delaying evaluation of high-grade dysplasia risks progression to invasive carcinoma.

questions.questionText:
A previously healthy 54-year-old man comes to the emergency department at his wife's insistence 6 days after a stray 
dog sprang up and bit his right leg while he and his wife were walking near the dog during a trip to South America. 
The bite punctured the skin. He immediately cleaned the wound thoroughly with soap and peroxide and has done so 
daily  since  the  incident  occurred.  The  area  of  the  bite  is  not  painful,  and  the  patient  has  not  had  fever  or  chills.  He  
takes no medications. He had a tetanus booster vaccination 3 years ago. Vital signs today are normal. Examination of 
the right lower extremity shows healing bite puncture wounds. There is minimal erythema and the area is not fluctuant. 
Lymph nodes in the groin are not palpable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.D: Order an MRI of the brain and spine
choices.E: No intervention is necessary at this time

explanations.A: This is correct. Any animal bite that punctures the skin in a country where canine rabies is endemic requires post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP), which includes both rabies immune globulin (on day 0) and the rabies vaccine series. The incubation period for rabies can be weeks to months, so initiating PEP even 6 days after exposure is still effective and strongly indicated.
explanations.B: Tetanus immune globulin is indicated for unvaccinated patients or those with unknown vaccination status who sustain a tetanus-prone wound. This patient had a tetanus booster only 3 years ago, so he is fully protected against tetanus and does not need immune globulin.
explanations.C: CSF analysis is used to diagnose meningitis or encephalitis, which would be relevant if clinical rabies had already developed. At this point the patient has no neurological symptoms; the priority is prevention via post-exposure prophylaxis before viral dissemination occurs.
explanations.D: Brain and spine MRI is an imaging study used when neurological disease is already present. There are no signs of neurological involvement here, and imaging would not prevent rabies infection. It would delay the critical window for effective post-exposure prophylaxis.
explanations.E: No intervention is inappropriate given the high-risk exposure. An unprovoked bite from a stray dog in a region endemic for rabies is a clear indication for rabies PEP regardless of the wound's current benign appearance or the patient's current lack of symptoms.

questions.questionText:
A  35-year-old  man  with  hypertension  comes  to  the  office  because  of  high  fever,  malaise,  and  arthralgias  during  the  
past 4 to 5 days. He also mentions having painless red bumps on the palms of both hands during the past few days. His 
current  medications  are  lisinopril  and  aspirin.  He  denies  any  alcohol  or  tobacco  use  but  admits  to  the  daily  use  of  
intravenous  heroin  during  the  past  month.  He  has  been  careful  to  use  clean  needles  except  for  one  incident  2  weeks  
ago  in  which  he  shared  needles  with  his  girlfriend.  His  most  recent  heroin  shot  was  yesterday.  Vital  signs  today  are  
temperature  39.8°C  (103.6°F),  pulse  120/min,  respirations  20/min,  and  blood  pressure  110/68  mm  Hg.  The  patient  
appears weak and pale. Skin is warm and moist. Small, nontender lymph nodes are palpable in both axillae and in the 
anterior neck and supraclavicular regions. There is a new, grade 2/6 holosystolic murmur heard best below the xiphoid 
that radiates to the apex and is increased slightly during inspiration. There are several small, nontender, erythematous 
nodules  on  the  palms  of  both  hands.  Physical  examination  is  otherwise  normal.  Based  on  the  physical  findings,  the  
most likely cause of his fever is infection with which of the following?

explanations.A: Hepatitis B infection can cause fever and malaise and is a risk with needle-sharing, but it does not produce the cardiac findings seen here. The new holosystolic murmur loudest at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration (Carvallo's sign) and the Janeway lesions on the palms point to a different diagnosis.
explanations.B: Acute HIV seroconversion (acute retroviral syndrome) can cause fever, lymphadenopathy, arthralgias, and a maculopapular rash, but it does not cause cardiac valvular disease or Janeway lesions. The tricuspid murmur is a hallmark of a bacterial rather than viral etiology in this clinical context.
explanations.C: Serratia marcescens is an opportunistic gram-negative rod that can cause bacteremia and endocarditis in IV drug users, but it is a much less common cause than Staphylococcus aureus. The constellation of findings here (right-sided murmur, Janeway lesions) is classic for S. aureus endocarditis.
explanations.D: This is correct. Staphylococcus aureus is the most common cause of acute infective endocarditis in IV drug users. The holosystolic murmur increasing with inspiration is consistent with tricuspid regurgitation (right-sided endocarditis). The painless erythematous palmar nodules are Janeway lesions — septic embolic phenomena pathognomonic of acute bacterial endocarditis — distinguishing them from the painful Osler nodes of subacute disease.
explanations.E: Treponema pallidum (syphilis) can cause a diffuse rash including palmar lesions in secondary syphilis, but these are not specifically erythematous nodules (Janeway lesions). Secondary syphilis does not produce acute valvular endocarditis, high fever of 39.8°C, or the murmur pattern described.

questions.questionText:
A 46-year-old married woman comes to the emergency department because of increased vaginal bleeding for 2 days. 
She says her menses have been irregular during the past 6 months and that she has not had a menstrual period for more 
than 2 months. She thinks she may be going through menopause. She says she has a history of leiomyomata uteri but 
has  not  had  an  operation  or  taken  medication.  Vital  signs  are  normal.  Abdominal  examination  is  normal.  Pelvic  
examination is consistent with an 8-week-sized uterus, which is slightly tender. A 5-cm right adnexal mass is palpated. 
The  cervical  os  is  closed,  and  there  are  small  clots  in  the  vaginal  vault.  Which  of  the  following  next  steps  is  most  
appropriate?

explanations.A: Dilation and curettage (D&C) is a procedural intervention that should not be performed before excluding ectopic pregnancy, as it would be dangerous and inappropriate if an ectopic is present. An adnexal mass combined with amenorrhea and vaginal bleeding mandates ruling out ectopic pregnancy before any uterine instrumentation.
explanations.B: Endometrial biopsy is used to evaluate abnormal uterine bleeding for endometrial pathology, but it would be premature and potentially dangerous here before a pregnancy test is obtained. Performing intrauterine procedures when an ectopic pregnancy may be present risks catastrophic outcomes.
explanations.C: Intravenous conjugated estrogen is used to manage acute non-pregnant dysfunctional uterine bleeding. Administering it before ruling out ectopic pregnancy is inappropriate; this patient's presentation — missed periods, adnexal mass, and vaginal bleeding — requires pregnancy testing first.
explanations.D: Pelvic ultrasonography is a valuable next step and will be needed to evaluate the adnexal mass and uterus, but it does not replace the serum pregnancy test as the immediate first step. A quantitative beta-hCG is faster, cheaper, and will direct the interpretation of any subsequent ultrasound findings.
explanations.E: This is correct. The combination of amenorrhea, irregular bleeding, an adnexal mass, uterine tenderness, and a closed cervical os is a classic presentation of possible ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise. A serum beta-hCG is the single most important immediate test; a positive result in the context of an adnexal mass would demand urgent evaluation for ectopic pregnancy.

questions.setVignette:
A 57-year-old man who manages his own accounting firm has a 5-year history of malignant melanoma that has been treated 
with local excision and immunotherapy. He now is admitted to the hospital for evaluation of constant pain in his back and left 
hip  and  an  11-kg  (24-lb)  weight  loss.  He  and  his  wife  of  35  years  are  worried  that  "the  cancer  may  be  back."  Pelvic  and  
abdominal CT scans show multiple bony metastases. He tells you, "I just want to die. I can't bear this."
questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial intervention?

choices.B: Arrange for him to be transferred to a psychiatric service
choices.E: Refer him to a cancer patient support group

explanations.A: This is correct. The patient has metastatic melanoma with bone pain that is described as constant — undertreated pain is the most pressing immediate clinical problem and should be addressed first. The WHO analgesic ladder dictates stepwise escalation of analgesia; adequate pain control is foundational to all other care and can also improve appetite and mood secondarily.
explanations.B: Psychiatric transfer is not appropriate at this stage. While depression is common in patients with terminal cancer, the immediate problem is uncontrolled pain. Transferring to psychiatry before addressing physical suffering would be both medically inappropriate and ethically problematic.
explanations.C: Beginning an antidepressant before addressing pain control would be premature. Many of the depressive-appearing symptoms in this patient (anorexia, low energy, withdrawal) may be driven by undertreated pain. The first step is to optimize analgesia; reassessment for a distinct depressive syndrome can occur after pain is controlled.
explanations.D: Hyperalimentation (parenteral nutrition) is not indicated for cancer-related weight loss in this context and carries significant risks including infection and metabolic complications. Weight loss from metastatic melanoma is largely due to cancer cachexia and pain-related anorexia, not a deficiency correctable by forced nutrition.
explanations.E: A support group can be a valuable adjunct but is not the most appropriate initial intervention in a patient suffering from uncontrolled bone pain. Psychosocial support is a secondary priority; physical symptom management — particularly pain — must come first.

questions.questionText: Which of the following symptoms would be most suggestive of a major depressive syndrome in this patient?

explanations.A: Anorexia is a vegetative symptom expected in both the medical illness itself (metastatic melanoma with bone pain) and in major depression, making it non-discriminating in this setting. Because it is attributable to the underlying cancer or pain, it cannot reliably identify a superimposed depressive syndrome.
explanations.B: Expressions of discouragement are a psychologically understandable and normative response to a terminal diagnosis with pain. Sadness and discouragement in the context of advanced cancer do not by themselves indicate a major depressive episode, which requires a more pervasive and qualitatively distinct mood disturbance.
explanations.C: Insomnia is another symptom shared between the pain state and major depression; uncontrolled bone pain would independently cause sleep disruption. Like anorexia, it lacks diagnostic specificity in this medically ill patient and cannot be used alone to diagnose a distinct psychiatric syndrome.
explanations.D: Low energy is nearly universal in patients with metastatic cancer experiencing significant weight loss and undertreated pain. As with anorexia and insomnia, it cannot distinguish a superimposed major depressive disorder from the expected physical sequelae of advanced malignancy.
explanations.E: This is correct. Withdrawal from family is a psychological/behavioral symptom not directly attributable to the physical illness or its treatment, making it the most diagnostically specific indicator of major depression in this patient. Social withdrawal that goes beyond the expected adjustment reaction — particularly if associated with hopelessness, worthlessness, or suicidal ideation — distinguishes major depressive disorder from the normative grief and adjustment seen in terminal illness.

questions.questionText:
A  25-year-old  woman  has  just  had  a  second  spontaneous  abortion  after  8  weeks  of  pregnancy.  She  has  also  had  an  
elective abortion in the past. She has been told that her uterus is in a retroflexed position and this finding is confirmed 
on  pelvic  examination.  The  rest  of  the  examination  is  normal.  Which  of  the  following  is  the  most  appropriate  
counseling?

choices.A:
Cervical  incompetence  is  frequently  associated  with  uterine  malposition  and  is  a  likely  contributor  to  
spontaneous abortion
choices.B:
Progesterone  therapy  is  likely  to  decrease  the  risk  for  recurrent  abortion  and  should  be  started  as  soon  as  
pregnancy is diagnosed
choices.C:
Renal  abnormalities  are  frequently  associated  with  uterine  malposition,  making  it  less  likely  that  successful  
pregnancy can be expected
choices.D: Uterine malposition should be corrected by surgical suspension prior to further attempts at pregnancy
choices.E: The  uterine  position  does  not  increase  her  risk  for  spontaneous  abortion,  and  therefore  no  specific  therapy  is

explanations.A: Cervical incompetence is not associated with uterine retroflexion. Cervical incompetence typically presents as painless mid-trimester losses (14–24 weeks), not first-trimester losses at 8 weeks. This patient's losses were early and the mechanism is unrelated to cervical structural deficiency.
explanations.B: Routine progesterone supplementation to prevent recurrent miscarriage is not supported by evidence for women with a retroflexed uterus, which itself is not a pathological finding. Progesterone may have a role in women with documented luteal phase defects or prior preterm birth but is not indicated here without a specific indication.
explanations.C: Renal anomalies are associated with Mullerian structural anomalies such as a bicornuate or septate uterus, not with uterine retroflexion. A retroflexed uterus is a positional variant and not a Mullerian fusion defect, so renal evaluation is not warranted.
explanations.D: Surgical uterine suspension (ventrosuspension) is not indicated for a retroflexed uterus prior to pregnancy. Retroflexion is a common normal variant and is not a recognized cause of spontaneous abortion; operating on the uterus would confer procedural risk without benefit.
explanations.E: This is correct. A retroflexed (or retroverted) uterus is a normal anatomical variant present in approximately 20–25% of women and is not associated with an increased risk of miscarriage. The two early pregnancy losses are not explained by uterine position; no specific therapy related to the malposition is needed, and counseling should reflect this reassuring fact.

questions.questionText:
A  5-year-old  boy  is  brought  to  the  office  by  his  mother  because  of  recurrence  of  bed-wetting  at  night.  He  has  a  3-
month-old sister who is healthy. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Results of urinalysis are shown: 
 
Specific gravity 1.010 
Glucose                           Negative                           
Protein                            Negative                            
Microscopic 0–1 WBC/hpf, 0 RBC/hpf 
 
Which of the following is the most important information to share with his parents?

choices.A: The condition will cease if they reprimand him for deliberately wetting the bed
choices.B: The condition is self-limiting, and they should take care to lessen the emotional impact on their child
choices.C: The condition is a potentially serious problem and could represent chronic inflammation of the kidneys
choices.D: The condition may be a precursor of diabetes mellitus
choices.E: The condition signifies a serious underlying emotional disorder

explanations.A: Secondary enuresis in a 5-year-old is involuntary and not a willful behavior, so reprimanding the child is both ineffective and psychologically harmful. Punishment increases shame and anxiety, which can worsen enuresis. This advice is incorrect and should never be given.
explanations.B: This is correct. Secondary enuresis — resumption of bed-wetting after a period of dryness — in a young child is almost always a benign, self-limiting regression triggered by a psychosocial stressor, here the birth of a sibling. The urinalysis is entirely normal, excluding organic causes. Parental education should focus on avoiding punishment, maintaining a supportive environment, and reassuring them that the condition will resolve spontaneously.
explanations.C: The normal urinalysis — no proteinuria, no hematuria, no WBC casts, normal specific gravity — provides no evidence of renal disease. Characterizing this as a potentially serious kidney problem would cause unnecessary alarm and parental anxiety and is not supported by the clinical data.
explanations.D: The urinalysis shows no glucosuria and the clinical history is inconsistent with diabetes mellitus. Diabetes insipidus or mellitus would typically produce dilute urine (low specific gravity) or glucosuria, neither of which is present here. This suggestion is unsupported and misleading.
explanations.E: Serious underlying emotional disorders do not manifest as isolated secondary enuresis in an otherwise healthy child with a clear, common precipitant (new sibling). While enuresis can occasionally be a symptom of significant psychological stress, the most parsimonious explanation here is simple sibling rivalry-related regression, not a psychiatric disorder.

questions.questionText:
A 38-year-old Hispanic bank executive comes to the emergency department because of the sudden onset of shortness 
of  breath,  light-headedness,  diaphoresis,  and  weakness.  He  is  afebrile.  On  auscultation  of  the  lungs,  bilateral  basilar  
rales are heard. ECG is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanations.A: Acute pericarditis typically presents with sharp pleuritic chest pain that improves when leaning forward, and produces diffuse saddle-shaped ST elevation on ECG without the regional changes seen in MI. Bilateral basilar rales indicating pulmonary edema would not be expected with pericarditis alone.
explanations.B: Hyperventilation syndrome causes dyspnea and light-headedness through respiratory alkalosis, but does not produce bilateral basilar crackles or the ECG changes consistent with acute ischemia. It is a diagnosis of exclusion and should never be entertained without first ruling out an acute cardiac event.
explanations.C: This is correct. The sudden onset of dyspnea, diaphoresis, light-headedness, bilateral basilar rales (pulmonary edema), and the ECG showing ST elevation with Q-wave changes in a middle-aged man is the classic presentation of acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI). This is the most common life-threatening diagnosis in this scenario and requires immediate reperfusion therapy.
explanations.D: Pulmonary embolism (PE) can cause dyspnea and diaphoresis but classically produces pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, tachycardia, and ECG findings of right heart strain (S1Q3T3 pattern or sinus tachycardia), not the ST-elevation pattern with Q waves associated with myocardial infarction. Bilateral basilar rales would be unusual in PE.
explanations.E: Spontaneous pneumothorax causes unilateral pleuritic chest pain and decreased breath sounds on the affected side, not bilateral basilar crackles. It is most common in tall, thin young men and does not produce the ECG changes or the bilateral pulmonary edema pattern described here.

questions.questionText:
A 3-year-old white girl is brought to the office by her parents for a follow-up visit 48 hours after receiving a 5-TU PPD 
skin test. The test was done as part of a routine screening for enrollment in a homeless shelter. Physical examination 
shows  10  mm  of  induration  at  the  puncture  site;  the  examination  is  otherwise  normal.  The  parents  tell  you  they  are  
shocked by this finding since both of their skin tests were nonreactive. They say they were born in this country and tell 
you that their daughter has always been in good health. She has not had much medical care in the past 2 years but she 
has been healthy. Until moving into this shelter they had been "squatters" in vacant buildings. Which of the following 
is the most appropriate step at this time?

choices.A: Call her previous physician to obtain more history
choices.C: Order a test for HIV antibody
choices.E: Schedule gastric aspiration for culture on successive days

explanations.A: Calling a previous physician might provide helpful background history but is not the most important immediate clinical action. The PPD result is positive and valid; the next step in standard TB evaluation is to assess for active disease with a chest x-ray, regardless of prior physician records.
explanations.B: This is correct. In a 3-year-old child with a positive PPD (10 mm induration is positive in a child at high risk due to homeless shelter exposure and residence in vacant buildings), the immediate next step is a chest x-ray to distinguish latent TB infection (LTBI) from active pulmonary tuberculosis. This guides treatment decisions — isoniazid alone for LTBI vs. multi-drug therapy for active disease.
explanations.C: HIV testing may eventually be warranted if the child has other risk factors or if the TB does not respond to treatment, but it is not the first priority. The standard TB evaluation algorithm begins with a chest x-ray, and there is no specific clinical indication for HIV testing here as the immediate next step.
explanations.D: Repeating the PPD is not appropriate. The test was read correctly at 48 hours with clear, significant induration of 10 mm. Repeating a test that has already given a clear positive result would only delay management and is not indicated.
explanations.E: Gastric aspiration for mycobacterial culture is used to confirm active TB in young children who cannot produce sputum, but it is not the first step. A chest x-ray must be done first to assess for active pulmonary disease; if the x-ray is abnormal, then cultures (including gastric aspiration) would be pursued to confirm the diagnosis.

questions.setVignette:
A  10-year-old  girl,  who  has  been  undergoing  treatment  for  chronic  juvenile  rheumatoid  arthritis  for  the  past  3  years,  is  
brought  to  the  office  by  her  parents  because  of  painful  swelling  of  the  right  knee.  She  has  had  three  episodes  of  painless  
swelling  of  her  left  knee  and  ankle,  which  have  subsided  spontaneously  with  rest  and  aspirin  therapy.  She  has  used  no  
medications between episodes. Physical examination today discloses pronounced redness and warmth around the right knee, 
and a large effusion. Attempts at active and passive motion cause severe pain.
questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most appropriate step at this time?

explanations.A: This is correct. In a child with known JRA who develops a new painful joint effusion (distinct from the typical painless swelling of JRA), joint aspiration is the critical immediate step. It serves both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes: the fluid must be analyzed to exclude septic arthritis, which would be a surgical emergency requiring immediate washout. The new onset of pain in a previously painless condition is a red flag.
explanations.B: Serum ANA titer is useful at the time of initial JRA diagnosis to stratify the risk for uveitis (ANA-positive JRA carries higher uveitis risk), but it does not help evaluate the acute painful joint. The immediate concern is ruling out septic arthritis, not re-establishing the JRA diagnosis.
explanations.C: Serum rheumatoid factor is part of the initial JRA workup and its result is unlikely to change management of an acute painful joint effusion. The majority of childhood JRA is RF-negative; a positive or negative result here would not address the immediate question of whether this joint is infected.
explanations.D: A technetium bone scan can show increased uptake in areas of inflammation or infection, but it lacks specificity and would not distinguish JRA flare from septic arthritis. It is not the appropriate immediate step when the joint needs to be evaluated for infection.
explanations.E: X-rays of the joint in JRA typically show soft tissue swelling, periarticular osteopenia, and in long-standing disease, erosions — none of which would differentiate an acute JRA flare from septic arthritis. Plain films cannot assess joint fluid and would not guide the immediate management decision.

questions.questionText:
Which  of  the  following  new  symptoms  or  findings,  if  present,  would  most  strongly  indicate  the  need  for  further  
diagnostic studies?

choices.B: Diffuse increase in technetium 99m uptake around the knee on bone scan
choices.C: Positive Gram stain of joint fluid

explanations.A: Decreased viscosity of joint fluid is actually expected in inflammatory arthritis (including JRA flares), because inflammatory mediators degrade hyaluronic acid. This finding would not necessitate additional urgent diagnostic studies beyond what is already being done for this child's known JRA.
explanations.B: Diffuse increased technetium uptake around the knee is a non-specific finding seen in any inflammatory joint condition including JRA flare, and would not on its own trigger additional studies beyond confirming the underlying diagnosis already established.
explanations.C: This is correct. A positive Gram stain of joint fluid would definitively indicate septic arthritis — a true orthopedic emergency requiring immediate surgical irrigation and debridement plus intravenous antibiotics. In a child with JRA, septic arthritis can be rapidly joint-destructive and life-threatening; this finding mandates urgent further diagnostic workup (including culture and sensitivities) and immediate surgical consultation.
explanations.D: A positive serum rheumatoid factor would confirm seropositive JRA, but most childhood JRA is seronegative. A positive RF does not indicate a change in the acute management of this knee and would not trigger additional urgent diagnostic studies over and above routine JRA monitoring.
explanations.E: Soft-tissue swelling on x-ray is a common, expected, and non-specific finding in any inflammatory arthritis, including JRA. It confirms the presence of a joint effusion but does not indicate infection and would not by itself prompt additional urgent diagnostic studies.

questions.questionText:
A 31-year-old white woman comes to the office for initial prenatal care. She is 12 weeks pregnant by date of her last 
menstrual  period.  This  is  her  fourth  pregnancy;  she  has  three  healthy  children.  Her  last  delivery  was  by  cesarean  
section  because  of  fetal  distress  during  labor.  Her  history  includes  heavy  use  of  alcohol  and  cigarettes,  and  multiple  
sexual partners. In addition to routine prenatal laboratory work-up, the patient consents to an HIV antibody test. The 
tests  are  ordered.  Later,  the  HIV  test  is  reported  as  positive.  At  a  follow-up  visit  the  patient  should  be  counseled  
regarding which of the following?

choices.A: Amniocentesis is recommended to rule out congenital HIV infection
choices.B: Breast-feeding will increase the risk for transmitting HIV to the infant
choices.C: Immediate termination of pregnancy will decrease her risk for progression to AIDS
choices.D: Repeat cesarean delivery may increase the risk for vertical transmission of HIV
choices.E: The risk for perinatal HIV transmission is greater than 50%

explanations.A: Amniocentesis does not diagnose congenital HIV infection; HIV is an RNA retrovirus that is not integrated into fetal chromosomes in a way detectable by amniocentesis, and the procedure itself carries a small risk of procedural transmission of HIV from mother to fetus. Neonatal HIV testing with PCR after birth is the appropriate diagnostic approach.
explanations.B: This is correct. HIV is present in breast milk and breast-feeding by an HIV-infected mother increases the risk of vertical (mother-to-child) transmission by approximately 14–22% in the absence of antiretroviral therapy. In resource-rich countries where safe formula feeding is available, HIV-positive women are counseled to avoid breast-feeding entirely to minimize transmission risk.
explanations.C: Termination of pregnancy does not reduce the risk of progression to AIDS for the mother. Disease progression depends on viral load, CD4 count, and antiretroviral therapy, not on pregnancy. Antiretroviral therapy (ART) is the intervention that reduces progression and, critically, reduces perinatal transmission.
explanations.D: Elective cesarean delivery at 38 weeks, when indicated, actually decreases the risk of vertical HIV transmission, particularly when maternal viral load is detectable. Repeat cesarean delivery does not increase vertical transmission risk; it may reduce it if performed before rupture of membranes and labor.
explanations.E: Without antiretroviral therapy, the risk of perinatal transmission of HIV is approximately 15–30%, not greater than 50%. With optimal ART and avoidance of breast-feeding, transmission rates can be reduced to less than 1–2%, which is the critical counseling point regarding why ART must be initiated.

questions.questionText:
While  you  are  on  rounds  at  a  local  nursing  facility,  the  nurse  mentions  that  your  patient,  a  79-year-old  African  
American  woman,  appears  to  be  a  "poor  eater."  She  was  admitted  to  the  nursing  facility  3  months  ago  from  the  
hospital where she was treated for congestive heart failure. Her daughter had moved away from the area, and nursing 
home placement was necessary because the patient could no longer function independently. Her present medications 
include  furosemide  and  digoxin.  Physical  examination  is  normal  except  for  a  weight  loss  of  3.5  kg  (7  lb)  during  the  
past  3  months.  In  your  conversation  with  the  patient,  she  says,  "No,  I'm  not  depressed,  I  just  don't  have  an  appetite  
anymore. Nothing tastes good to me. I have a little bit of nausea most of the time." Which of the following is the most 
appropriate initial diagnostic study?

explanations.A: A chest x-ray could evaluate for pulmonary congestion from CHF, but the patient's primary symptoms — nausea, anorexia, and weight loss — are not typical of decompensated heart failure. These symptoms in a patient on digoxin should prompt evaluation for drug toxicity before more distant etiologies.
explanations.B: A complete blood count could detect anemia, which might contribute to anorexia or fatigue, but it would not address the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms. Anemia would not explain the chronic nausea that is the predominant complaint here.
explanations.C: Serum albumin concentration assesses nutritional status and can confirm malnutrition, but it is a consequence of poor intake rather than a cause. Knowing the albumin level would not change the immediate diagnostic priority of identifying why this patient is not eating.
explanations.D: This is correct. This patient has the classic symptoms of digoxin toxicity: nausea, anorexia, and dysgeusia (food tasting different/bad). Digoxin has a narrow therapeutic index, and elderly patients on concomitant furosemide are at particularly high risk because diuretic-induced hypokalemia potentiates digoxin toxicity. The serum digoxin level is the most appropriate and directed first diagnostic step.
explanations.E: An upper GI barium study could evaluate structural causes of nausea and anorexia, but it is an invasive study that should not precede a simple serum drug level when digoxin toxicity is the most clinically compelling explanation. Pursuing imaging before checking the digoxin level would be inefficient and potentially delay recognition of toxicity.

questions.setVignette:
A 28-year-old white woman returns to the office for follow-up of hypertension. She says, "My blood pressure keeps getting 
worse, no matter what I do." Her hypertension had been controlled with a diuretic and a β-blocking medication for 4 years, 
but  her  blood  pressure  has  steadily  increased  in  the  past  8  months  despite  taking  maximum  doses  of  the  medications.  She  
insists  she  takes  her  medications  as  directed.  Family  history  is  negative  for  hypertension.  She  does  not  smoke  cigarettes.  
Height  is  163 cm (5 ft 4 in)  and  weight  is  75 kg (165 lb);  BMI  is  28 kg/m

.  Vital  signs  today  are  pulse  96/min  and  blood  
pressure  165/110  mm  Hg,  standing,  in  both  arms.  Examination  of  the  retina  shows  AV  nicking.  Abdominal  examination  
discloses a new epigastric bruit. The remainder of the examination is normal.
questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic study to order next?

choices.A: 24-Hour urine collection for creatinine clearance and protein excretion
choices.B: 24-Hour urine collection for vanillylmandelic acid, metanephrine and catecholamine excretion
choices.D: Serum thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), cortisol, and aldosterone concentrations
choices.E: Urinalysis, and serum creatinine and urea nitrogen concentrations

explanations.A: A 24-hour urine for creatinine clearance and protein evaluates renal function and proteinuria, which are relevant in established renal disease. However, this does not address the initial screening needed to determine whether this young woman has renal parenchymal disease, renal artery stenosis, or another cause of secondary hypertension. Simpler initial tests come first.
explanations.B: A 24-hour urine for VMA, metanephrines, and catecholamines screens for pheochromocytoma. While this is important in a young patient with resistant hypertension, the most common secondary cause in a young woman with progressively worsening hypertension despite multiple medications is renovascular disease (renal artery stenosis), which should be assessed first.
explanations.C: Serum and urine electrolytes screen for primary hyperaldosteronism (hypokalemia, high urine potassium). While this is a consideration in resistant hypertension, the most common and clinically pertinent first-line evaluation in a young woman with worsening hypertension is a basic assessment of kidney function.
explanations.D: TSH, cortisol, and aldosterone screen for thyroid disease, Cushing syndrome, and hyperaldosteronism respectively. These are all on the differential for secondary hypertension, but the initial evaluation should begin with the most basic assessment — renal function and urinalysis — before moving to hormonal studies.
explanations.E: This is correct. The initial workup for resistant hypertension in any patient should begin with urinalysis and serum creatinine/BUN to assess for renal parenchymal disease, which is the most common cause of secondary hypertension. These tests are non-invasive, inexpensive, and rapidly available, and will direct whether further evaluation for renal artery stenosis or other etiologies is warranted.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most appropriate imaging study?

explanations.A: Abdominal CT with contrast can visualize the kidneys and renal vessels but uses ionizing radiation and nephrotoxic contrast, and is not the preferred first-line imaging for renal artery stenosis. CT angiography is a later-step study once noninvasive evaluation suggests RAS.
explanations.B: Abdominal ultrasonography evaluates renal size and structure and can detect parenchymal disease, but standard B-mode ultrasound does not visualize blood flow in the renal arteries. It would not adequately evaluate for renal artery stenosis, which is the suspected cause of this young woman's resistant hypertension.
explanations.C: Adrenal MRI is indicated when adrenal pathology such as pheochromocytoma or aldosterone-producing adenoma is suspected. Given the clinical picture of a young woman with worsening hypertension — more consistent with fibromuscular dysplasia of the renal arteries — adrenal imaging is not the highest-yield next imaging step.
explanations.D: Radionuclide thyroid scan is used to evaluate thyroid nodules and hyperthyroidism, and has no role in the evaluation of resistant hypertension unless clinical and biochemical evidence of thyroid disease has been established. It is not relevant to the most likely diagnosis here.
explanations.E: This is correct. Renal duplex (Doppler) ultrasonography is the preferred first-line noninvasive imaging study to evaluate for renovascular hypertension (renal artery stenosis). It can detect elevated renal artery flow velocities and resistive indices consistent with stenosis. In young women with resistant hypertension, fibromuscular dysplasia of the renal arteries is the most common cause and renal duplex ultrasound is the appropriate initial imaging modality.

questions.questionText:
A 27-year-old nulligravid woman returns to the office to discuss results of a Pap smear obtained 2 weeks ago during a 
health  maintenance  examination.  At  that  time,  physical  examination,  including  pelvic  examination,  disclosed  no  
abnormalities. The patient's menstrual periods occur at regular 28-day intervals. She has been in a stable relationship 
with  the  same  man  for  3  years  and  she  uses  a  diaphragm  with  spermicidal  jelly  for  contraception.  A  Pap  smear  
obtained  3  years  ago  showed  no  abnormalities.  Pap  smear  obtained  at  her  last  visit  shows  evidence  of  marked  
inflammation  suggestive  of  a  high-grade  squamous  intraepithelial  lesion.  Which  of  the  following  is  the  most  
appropriate next step?

choices.A: Advise the patient that her partner should use condoms for contraception and repeat the Pap smear in 3 months
choices.B: Do colposcopic examination of the cervix after application of 5% acetic acid solution
choices.D: Reassure the patient and repeat the Pap smear in 3 months
choices.E: Treat the patient with metronidazole for 2 weeks and repeat the Pap smear in 3 months

explanations.A: Switching to condoms does not address the high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (HSIL) on Pap smear and simply repeating the Pap in 3 months would delay necessary diagnostic evaluation. HSIL is not managed expectantly with repeat smears; it requires immediate colposcopic evaluation per established guidelines.
explanations.B: This is correct. A Pap smear showing HSIL requires immediate colposcopic evaluation — this is standard of care per ASCCP guidelines. Colposcopy with application of 5% acetic acid allows directed biopsies of acetowhite lesions on the cervix to determine the actual histologic grade of the lesion (CIN II, CIN III, or invasive cancer) and guide definitive treatment decisions.
explanations.C: Cervical conization (cold knife or LEEP) is a therapeutic/diagnostic excisional procedure that may ultimately be needed for HSIL/CIN III, but it should not be done before colposcopy with directed biopsy confirms the diagnosis and the extent of the lesion. Performing conization without prior colposcopy would be overly aggressive and bypass the diagnostic step.
explanations.D: Reassuring and simply repeating the Pap smear in 3 months is appropriate only for low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (LSIL) or atypical squamous cells of undetermined significance (ASC-US) in certain circumstances. An HSIL finding requires immediate colposcopic evaluation and cannot be managed with watchful waiting.
explanations.E: Metronidazole treats bacterial vaginosis or trichomoniasis. While the Pap described inflammation, the finding is HSIL, not simply reactive atypia from inflammation. Treating for a vaginal infection and repeating the smear would be an inappropriate response to an HSIL result, which represents a significant premalignant lesion requiring direct cervical evaluation.

questions.questionText:
A  46-year-old  man  with  Marfan  syndrome,  aortic  insufficiency,  and  mitral  regurgitation  comes  to  the  emergency  
department  because  he  has  had  severe  substernal  chest  pain  for  the  past  3  hours.  He  describes  the  pain  as  tearing  in  
quality  and  radiating  to  the  neck.  One  week  earlier  he  experienced  similar,  but  less  severe  chest  pain  and  treated  
himself with aspirin. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause for his worsening symptoms?

explanations.A: Acute bacterial endocarditis can occur in Marfan syndrome due to underlying valvular disease, but it would present with fever, embolic phenomena, and new murmurs, not with acute tearing chest pain. The pain quality here is more characteristic of a vascular catastrophe than an infectious process.
explanations.B: Acute myocardial infarction causes substernal chest pain, but it is typically described as pressure, squeezing, or crushing — not tearing — and does not radiate to the neck in this characteristic fashion. Marfan patients are at risk for aortic pathology from their connective tissue defect, which is the far more likely diagnosis given the pain quality.
explanations.C: This is correct. The combination of Marfan syndrome (connective tissue disorder causing cystic medial necrosis of the aorta), severe tearing chest pain radiating to the neck, and a history of similar but milder antecedent pain one week earlier is the classic presentation of aortic dissection. Marfan syndrome is one of the strongest risk factors for Type A aortic dissection, which is a surgical emergency.
explanations.D: Esophageal reflux with spasm can cause substernal chest pain and even mimic cardiac pain, but the quality described — tearing, radiating to the neck, in a patient with Marfan syndrome and aortic regurgitation — is not consistent with an esophageal etiology. This diagnosis would not explain the severity or quality of pain.
explanations.E: Perforated peptic ulcer causes sudden severe epigastric pain with peritoneal signs (board-like rigidity, rebound tenderness) and free air under the diaphragm. It would not explain tearing substernal pain radiating to the neck, and would not be specifically associated with Marfan syndrome and pre-existing aortic valve disease.

questions.questionText:
A 30-year-old man who has been your patient for several years comes to the office for a periodic health evaluation. He 
has been healthy, but has a birth defect involving his hands and feet. He is missing the second and third metatarsals, 
metacarpals, and corresponding fingers and toes. He has three healthy children, and no one else in the family has this 
condition.  He  has  adapted  well  to  his  condition.  During  your  evaluation,  he  asks,  "What  do  you  think?  If  I  have  any  
more kids, will they have hands and feet like mine?" You review the family history and his pedigree, which is shown. 
His  physical  examination  is  otherwise  normal.  In  addition  to  discussing  your  opinions  with  him,  which  of  the  
following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.A: Do a skin biopsy for fibroblast culture
choices.B: Order chromosome analysis of peripheral blood lymphocytes
choices.C: Order radionuclide scan of his hands and feet
choices.D: Order skeletal x-ray surveys of his three children
choices.E: Order no additional tests at this time

explanations.A: Fibroblast culture from a skin biopsy is used for biochemical enzyme assays in suspected metabolic or lysosomal storage diseases, not for evaluating a skeletal limb deficiency. This patient has a clearly defined structural anomaly (absent rays) and the pedigree showing apparent de novo occurrence; fibroblast culture would not provide useful information about recurrence risk.
explanations.B: Peripheral blood karyotype would detect chromosomal aneuploidy or large structural chromosomal rearrangements, but the defect described — absence of specific digital rays — is more consistent with a single-gene or developmental field defect, not a chromosomal syndrome. The pedigree and normal examination make a chromosomal cause unlikely.
explanations.C: A radionuclide scan of the hands and feet measures bone metabolism and blood flow, not structural anatomy. It would not provide any additional useful information about the structural limb deficiency or its inheritance pattern and is not used in genetic counseling evaluation.
explanations.D: Skeletal x-ray surveys of the children are not indicated because the pedigree (as shown) demonstrates a de novo occurrence with no affected family members across multiple generations. There is no clinical indication to subject healthy, presumably normal children to radiation-based testing when there is no family history of the condition.
explanations.E: This is correct. The pedigree shows an isolated case with no family history of the condition, consistent with a de novo mutation or a sporadically occurring developmental anomaly. In the context of an apparently sporadic limb deficiency with a normal pedigree and no known syndrome, genetic counseling (referral to a genetic counselor or medical geneticist) is the appropriate next step, and no additional laboratory or imaging tests are warranted at this time.

questions.questionText:
A  75-year-old  woman  comes  to  the  office  because  she  has  band-like,  burning  pain  in  her  right  upper  abdomen  
extending  from  the  epigastrium  around  to  the  midline  of  the  back.  Physical  examination  discloses  no  abdominal  
tenderness  to  palpation.  Findings  on  ultrasonography  of  the  gallbladder  are  normal.  Serum  amylase  concentration  is  
within the reference range. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanations.A: Acalculous cholecystitis is an inflammatory condition of the gallbladder without stones, typically occurring in critically ill patients. However, this patient's gallbladder ultrasonography is explicitly described as normal, making any gallbladder pathology unlikely, and the band-like dermatological distribution of the pain is inconsistent with biliary disease.
explanations.B: Chronic relapsing pancreatitis can cause band-like epigastric pain radiating to the back, and classically the pain radiates through to the back in a belt-like distribution. However, the serum amylase is within normal range and the normal ultrasound argues against pancreatitis, while the key clinical feature — dermatomal distribution of burning pain — points to a different etiology.
explanations.C: Cecal diverticulitis typically causes right lower quadrant pain, not upper abdominal and mid-back band-like pain. The location and quality of this pain (band-like, burning, from epigastrium around to back) is not consistent with colonic diverticular disease.
explanations.D: This is correct. The classic presentation of herpes zoster (shingles) is a unilateral dermatomal band-like burning pain, often preceding the vesicular rash by several days. The location (right upper abdomen extending to mid-back) corresponds to a thoracic dermatome, and the absence of abdominal tenderness on palpation distinguishes it from visceral pathology. Zoster is more common and severe in elderly patients and should always be considered when pain follows a dermatomal distribution.
explanations.E: A penetrating duodenal ulcer can cause epigastric pain that radiates to the back, but it would typically be associated with a history of peptic ulcer disease, tenderness on palpation, and an elevated amylase if the pancreas is involved. The band-like burning quality and dermatomal distribution described here are not characteristic of peptic ulcer disease.

questions.questionText:
A 19-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because, she says, "I'm burning up." She is known to staff 
as an intravenous drug user. Physical examination discloses a systolic heart murmur over the precordium. An expected 
physical finding will be which of the following?

choices.B: Increased intensity of the murmur with deep inspiration
choices.C: Increased intensity of the murmur with forced expiration
choices.D: Positive Kussmaul sign (rise in jugular venous pulse with inspiration)

explanations.A: Decreased intensity of S1 occurs with mitral regurgitation (left-sided) when the valve leaflets do not coapt fully, or with conditions that reduce left ventricular contractility. In an IV drug user with fever and a murmur, right-sided endocarditis (tricuspid) is most likely, and changes in S1 intensity are not the expected auscultatory finding.
explanations.B: This is correct. The murmur of tricuspid regurgitation — the most common valvular lesion in right-sided infective endocarditis in IV drug users — characteristically increases in intensity with deep inspiration (Carvallo's sign). Inspiration increases venous return to the right heart, augmenting right ventricular volume and flow across the tricuspid valve, thereby increasing the regurgitant murmur.
explanations.C: Increased intensity with forced expiration is characteristic of left-sided murmurs. Expiration reduces venous return to the right side of the heart and increases left heart filling. Right-sided murmurs (including tricuspid regurgitation) behave in the opposite manner and decrease with expiration.
explanations.D: Kussmaul's sign (paradoxical rise in JVP with inspiration) is seen in constrictive pericarditis and right ventricular infarction, conditions in which the right ventricle cannot accommodate the increased venous return during inspiration. It would not be an expected finding with tricuspid regurgitation from endocarditis, where the right side is volume-overloaded but not rigidly constrained.
explanations.E: A right-sided gallop (S3 or S4 at the left lower sternal border, increasing with inspiration) can occur in right heart failure, but it is not the expected hallmark physical finding in this clinical scenario. The more diagnostically specific expected finding in tricuspid regurgitation from right-sided endocarditis is the Carvallo sign (inspiratory increase of the murmur).

questions.questionText:
A 26-year-old woman comes to the office because of fever, cough, and increasing shortness of breath for the past 3 days. She has been living in homeless shelters and says she uses intravenous drugs. She recently tested positive for HIV infection. She takes no medications and has no history of asthma, pneumonia, or tuberculosis. Her last medical evaluation was 5 years ago. Vital signs are temperature 39.0°C (102.2°F), pulse 100/min, respirations 28/min, and blood pressure 110/60 mm Hg. Auscultation of the chest discloses crackles and rhonchi posteriorly over the right lower lung field with tubular breath sounds and dullness to percussion. No sputum could be obtained due to splinting of the chest wall. Chest x-ray shows consolidation of the right lower lobe. Complete blood count and arterial blood gas analysis while breathing room air show: 
Blood                              Arterial blood gas analysis
  Hematocrit    36%                  Po₂     72 mm Hg
  Hemoglobin    12.7 g/dL            Pco₂    33 mm Hg
  WBC           7800/mm³             pH      7.44
    Neutrophils, segmented  70%
    Neutrophils, bands      16%
    Lymphocytes             14%
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanations.A: Legionella pneumophila causes Legionnaires' disease, which typically presents with high fever, GI symptoms, hyponatremia, and diffuse patchy infiltrates on chest x-ray — often bilateral. It does not characteristically produce lobar consolidation of a single lobe, and it is diagnosed by urinary antigen testing, not typically by sputum Gram stain.
explanations.B: Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia (PCP) is a major opportunistic infection in HIV patients with low CD4 counts, but it characteristically presents with bilateral diffuse interstitial infiltrates on chest x-ray (not lobar consolidation), a gradual onset, severe hypoxia disproportionate to symptoms, and LDH elevation. PCP does not cause lobar consolidation.
explanations.C: This is correct. The clinical picture — acute onset of fever, productive-type presentation, lobar consolidation on chest x-ray, neutrophilia with bandemia (16% bands), and tubular breath sounds with dullness to percussion — is the classic presentation of community-acquired bacterial pneumonia, most likely caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae. Even in an HIV-positive patient, typical bacterial pneumonia is the most common cause of community-acquired lobar pneumonia and should be treated first.
explanations.D: Pulmonary embolism causes pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, and hypoxia, but does not produce lobar consolidation with consolidative breath sounds and bandemia. The CBC showing a left shift indicates a bacterial process, not thromboembolic disease, and the clinical picture is inconsistent with PE.
explanations.E: Pulmonary tuberculosis in HIV-positive patients can present atypically (lower lobe involvement, absence of cavitation), but TB typically has a more indolent course over weeks rather than 3 days. Additionally, TB does not produce the acute bandemia (16% bands) seen here, which reflects an acute pyogenic bacterial infection rather than mycobacterial disease.

questions.questionText:
A  60-year-old  man  had  a  total  thyroidectomy  and  excision  of  enlarged  left  jugular  lymph  nodes  for  follicular  
carcinoma.  The  operation  was  uncomplicated.  He  is  receiving  intravenous  5%  dextrose  and  0.45%  saline  with  
potassium. Twelve hours after the operation he develops circumoral numbness and paresthesias in his fingertips, and 
he  becomes  very  anxious.  Vital  signs  are  temperature  37.6°C  (99.7°F),  pulse  90/min,  respirations  16/min,  and  blood
