pressure  140/90 mm Hg.  Physical  examination  discloses  a  dry  neck  dressing  and  no  stridor.  Extremities  are  warm,  
with brisk capillary refill time. Additional physical examination is most likely to show which of the following?

choices.C: Deviation of the tongue to the left side

explanations.A: A Babinski sign (extensor plantar response) indicates upper motor neuron dysfunction affecting the corticospinal tract. While hypoparathyroidism can cause hyperreflexia in severe cases, Babinski signs are not expected in post-thyroidectomy hypocalcemia and would suggest a different neurological process.
explanations.B: This is correct. Circumoral numbness, fingertip paresthesias, and anxiety 12 hours after total thyroidectomy are classic signs of hypocalcemia due to inadvertent removal or devascularization of the parathyroid glands. Chvostek's sign — ipsilateral facial muscle twitching elicited by tapping the facial nerve anterior to the ear — is a classic bedside test for hypocalcemia-induced neuromuscular irritability and is the expected physical finding in this patient.
explanations.C: Deviation of the tongue to one side indicates damage to the hypoglossal nerve (CN XII). While hypoglossal nerve injury can occur during neck dissection, it is not the expected or most likely complication after total thyroidectomy with left jugular lymph node dissection, and does not explain the circumoral numbness or paresthesias.
explanations.D: A drooping left shoulder (inability to shrug) would indicate damage to the spinal accessory nerve (CN XI), which runs through the posterior triangle of the neck and is at risk during radical neck dissection. However, this finding would not explain the sensory symptoms (circumoral numbness, fingertip paresthesias) that are the cardinal manifestation of hypocalcemia.
explanations.E: Hyporeflexia is a sign of hypocalcemia that occurs only in rare circumstances and is not the characteristic finding. Rather, hypocalcemia classically causes hyperreflexia and increased neuromuscular excitability — the opposite of hyporeflexia. Hyporeflexia would instead suggest hypomagnesemia, hypothyroidism, or other metabolic derangements.

questions.questionText:
A 2-year-old African American child with sickle cell disease is brought to the emergency department by her parents because of painful swelling of her feet for the past 3 hours. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F). Physical examination shows swelling and tenderness of her feet; no other abnormal findings are noted. Results of laboratory studies are shown: 
Hemoglobin    7.8 g/dL
WBC           13,000/mm³
  Neutrophils, segmented  60%
  Lymphocytes             40%
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanations.A: This is correct. Dactylitis (hand-foot syndrome) is the classic presentation of vaso-occlusive crisis in young children with sickle cell disease, causing painful swelling of the hands and feet due to bone marrow infarction in the small bones. The afebrile status and normal differential (no left shift) argue against infection.
explanations.B: E. coli sepsis would present with fever, a left shift on CBC, and signs of systemic illness rather than isolated painful extremity swelling. This gram-negative organism is not a typical cause of the hand-foot syndrome pattern seen here.
explanations.C: Pneumococcal sepsis is a serious concern in asplenic sickle cell patients but typically presents with high fever, systemic toxicity, and bacteremia. This child is afebrile with an isolated finding of painful foot swelling, making sepsis unlikely.
explanations.D: Osteomyelitis in sickle cell disease is typically caused by Salmonella or Staphylococcus and presents with fever, local warmth, erythema, and a more insidious course. The afebrile presentation and bilateral symmetric involvement in a toddler point to vaso-occlusive dactylitis rather than infection.
explanations.E: Staphylococcal sepsis would produce fever, systemic inflammation, and likely a left shift on differential. The lack of fever, normal segmented neutrophil percentage (no bandemia), and the clinical picture of painful swelling in a young sickle cell child is characteristic of vaso-occlusive crisis, not bacterial sepsis.

questions.setVignette:
A 4-day-old female, Greek American newborn is brought to the office by her mother because the infant developed yellowing 
of her skin 1 day after hospital discharge and approximately 20 red spots over her skin yesterday. The infant has continued to 
feed  well.  Pregnancy  and  delivery  were  uncomplicated.  Birth  weight  was  3400 g (7 lb 8 oz; 50th percentile).  Apgar  scores 
were 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes, respectively. The mother, gravida 2, para 2, has blood group A, Rh-positive. The neonate's 
blood  group  is  O,  Rh-positive.  Result  of  a  direct  antiglobulin  (Coombs)  test  was  negative.  The  neonate  was  breast-feeding  
and  was  doing  well  at  the  time  of  discharge.  Weight  today  is  3250  g  (7  lb  2  oz;  25th  percentile).  Physical  examination  
discloses  jaundice  and  scleral  icterus.  Many  papules  containing  small  vesicles  with  clear  to  slightly  turbid  fluid  are  present  
over the newborn's face, trunk, and extremities. No organomegaly or adenopathy is noted. Serum total bilirubin concentration 
is 8.7 mg/dL, and serum conjugated (direct) bilirubin concentration is 0.7 mg/dL.
questions.questionText:
In  addition  to  scheduling  a  follow-up  visit  in  1  week,  which  of  the  following  is  the  most  appropriate  next  step  
regarding the newborn's jaundice?

choices.A: Advise the mother to avoid eating foods containing large quantities of carotene
choices.B: Begin administering small doses of phenobarbital to the newborn
choices.C: Recommend discontinuation of breast-feeding until the jaundice has disappeared
choices.D: Recommend home phototherapy for the newborn
choices.E: Recommend no change in child care or feeding of the newborn

explanations.A: Carotene causes carotenemia (yellowish skin discoloration), which is distinct from jaundice and does not elevate bilirubin. This recommendation is irrelevant and incorrect, as the infant's jaundice is physiologic and unrelated to the mother's dietary carotene intake.
explanations.B: Phenobarbital induces hepatic glucuronosyltransferase and can reduce bilirubin levels, but it is not indicated for physiologic jaundice in a well-appearing, well-feeding infant. This approach carries unnecessary pharmacologic risk when no intervention is needed.
explanations.C: Discontinuing breastfeeding is not recommended for physiologic jaundice; breastfeeding should be encouraged and continued. Breast milk jaundice is a separate entity that occurs later (after day 5-7) and requires a different management approach than physiologic jaundice.
explanations.D: Home phototherapy is reserved for infants whose bilirubin levels approach treatment thresholds based on age in hours and risk factors. A well-appearing, well-feeding 4-day-old with physiologic jaundice does not meet criteria for phototherapy without knowing the actual bilirubin level is dangerously elevated.
explanations.E: This is correct. The infant is a well-appearing Greek American neonate with jaundice appearing on day 3-4, which is consistent with normal physiologic jaundice. She is feeding well and has no signs of pathologic hyperbilirubinemia; watchful waiting with a follow-up visit in one week is the appropriate management.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most appropriate management for the newborn's rash?

choices.B: Daily wet-to-dry povidone-iodine (Betadine) soaks with gauze pads on each vesicle
choices.D: Routine skin care with soap and water
choices.E: Scrubbing daily with entsufon cleanser firmly enough to unroof the vesicles

explanations.A: Hydrocortisone cream is used for inflammatory skin conditions such as eczema or contact dermatitis. The rash described (approximately 20 red spots/vesicles) in a 4-day-old Greek American newborn is consistent with erythema toxicum neonatorum or miliaria, which are benign self-limited conditions requiring no steroid treatment.
explanations.B: Povidone-iodine soaks are used for infected or necrotic wounds and would be inappropriate and potentially harmful for a benign neonatal rash. Iodine absorption through neonatal skin can cause thyroid suppression, making this approach both unnecessary and unsafe.
explanations.C: Polymyxin antibiotic ointment is indicated for bacterial skin infections. The spotted vesicular rash in this otherwise healthy neonate is consistent with a benign condition (erythema toxicum or miliaria) with no evidence of bacterial infection.
explanations.D: This is correct. The rash in this well newborn is most consistent with erythema toxicum neonatorum or miliaria, both of which are benign, self-limited conditions requiring only routine skin care. No specific treatment is necessary, and simple hygiene with soap and water is the appropriate recommendation.
explanations.E: Unroofing vesicles would disrupt the skin barrier, introduce infection risk, and cause unnecessary pain to the infant. Erythema toxicum and miliaria resolve spontaneously; aggressive scrubbing would be harmful and entirely contraindicated.

questions.questionText:
A 6-month-old male, Hispanic infant is brought to the office by his parents because of intermittent swelling of his right 
scrotum that is more pronounced when he cries. The swelling has never been red or "stuck." Vital signs are normal. A 
right  inguinal  hernia  is  confirmed  on  physical  examination.  In  discussing  repair  of  the  hernia  with  the  parents,  you  
should inform them which of the following?

choices.A: Herniorrhaphy can be postponed until age 2 years because many hernias close spontaneously
choices.B: Herniorrhaphy can be postponed until age 12 years because oligospermia does not develop before age 12
choices.C: Herniorrhaphy should be scheduled at the earliest convenient time
choices.D: Herniorrhaphy should be scheduled as an emergency operation
choices.E: There is no need to repair the hernia in childhood unless incarceration occurs

explanations.A: Unlike hydroceles, indirect inguinal hernias in infants do not close spontaneously because they result from a patent processus vaginalis through which bowel can herniate. Delaying repair until age 2 significantly increases the risk of incarceration, which is highest in the first year of life.
explanations.B: There is no clinical basis for delaying herniorrhaphy until age 12. The risk of incarceration and strangulation exists from infancy, and concern for oligospermia relates to injury from an incarcerated hernia compressing the vas deferens or testicular vessels, which argues for earlier, elective repair.
explanations.C: This is correct. Indirect inguinal hernias in infants do not close spontaneously and carry a significant risk of incarceration (up to 30% in the first year of life). Elective repair should be scheduled promptly at the earliest convenient time to prevent the higher morbidity of emergency surgery for an incarcerated hernia.
explanations.D: Emergency herniorrhaphy is indicated for an incarcerated or strangulated hernia. This hernia is reducible (swelling resolves and has never been 'stuck'), so emergency surgery is not warranted; elective surgery scheduled at the earliest convenient time is the appropriate recommendation.
explanations.E: Watchful waiting until incarceration occurs is inappropriate because incarceration dramatically increases operative risk, can cause bowel necrosis, and may damage the vas deferens or testicular blood supply. Elective repair while the hernia is reducible is safer and preferred.

questions.questionText:
Three weeks ago a 45-year-old man was admitted to the hospital because of frostbite of both feet. He was treated by 
rapid  rewarming  and  protective  care  of  the  feet.  All  the  toes  on  the  right  foot  have  turned  black.  He  has  become  
slightly  febrile  and  progressively  more  confused  over  the  past  few  days.  Examination  discloses  cellulitis  in  the  
midfoot. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?

choices.C: Debridement   of   necrotic skin over the toes

explanations.A: This is correct. This patient has gangrenous toes (black discoloration), spreading cellulitis in the midfoot, fever, and progressive confusion, indicating sepsis from wet gangrene. These are clear signs of life-threatening infection requiring amputation; delayed demarcation is not appropriate when systemic sepsis is present.
explanations.B: Topical collagenase is used to debride necrotic tissue in stable, non-infected wounds. This patient has signs of systemic sepsis (fever, confusion, spreading cellulitis) that demand surgical intervention; topical enzymatic debridement would be wholly inadequate.
explanations.C: Debridement of necrotic skin alone would be insufficient given the extent of gangrene (all toes of the right foot) and the presence of spreading cellulitis and systemic sepsis. Piecemeal debridement without amputation risks ongoing source of infection and septic deterioration.
explanations.D: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy can support wound healing in selected frostbite or ischemic wound cases but is not the treatment of choice for established wet gangrene with systemic sepsis. Amputation to remove the source of infection is the priority when the patient is septic.
explanations.E: Whirlpool therapy is appropriate in the early acute phase of frostbite to aid in gentle rewarming and wound care. At three weeks post-injury with black gangrenous toes, cellulitis, fever, and confusion, the patient is beyond the stage where whirlpool therapy is appropriate.

questions.questionText:
A 44-year-old African American construction worker comes to the emergency department because of excruciating left 
flank pain that radiates to his left testicle. He describes the pain as occurring in waves and says, "This is the worst pain 
I've had in my life, and that includes closing my thumb in my truck door." He is extremely restless and is in obvious 
pain. Genitalia are normal. Abdominal examination discloses intermittent guarding with spasms of pain. Plain x-ray of 
the abdomen shows no abnormalities. Results of urinalysis are shown: 
 
pH                                    6.5                                    
Specific gravity 1.025 
Glucose                            Negative
Protein                              Negative
 
Urinary sediment is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic study?
questions.imageAlt: Urinary sediment microscopy showing calcium oxalate crystals

choices.D: Determination of serum uric acid concentration

explanations.A: This is correct. The clinical presentation (colicky flank pain radiating to the groin/testicle, restlessness, hematuria on urinalysis) with a normal plain abdominal x-ray is classic for nephrolithiasis. CT of the abdomen (non-contrast CT KUB) is the gold standard diagnostic study, detecting stones with >95% sensitivity and also evaluating for alternative diagnoses.
explanations.B: CT scan of just the kidney (rather than the full abdomen) would be incomplete, as ureteral calculi can be located anywhere from the renal pelvis to the ureterovesical junction. A full abdominal CT (CT KUB) is the appropriate study to visualize the entire urinary tract.
explanations.C: Urine culture is appropriate when infection is suspected (e.g., in obstructive uropathy with infected urine), but the urinalysis shown does not indicate infection. The priority is to confirm the diagnosis of nephrolithiasis and locate the stone, which requires imaging.
explanations.D: Serum uric acid would be relevant to evaluate for uric acid stones, but this is a secondary metabolic workup done after the acute episode is managed. It does not confirm the diagnosis of a stone or determine its location, which is the immediate clinical need.
explanations.E: 24-hour urinary calcium is a metabolic evaluation to determine stone-forming risk in patients with known or recurrent nephrolithiasis. It is a secondary study done after the acute episode and is not the most appropriate immediate diagnostic study for this presentation.

questions.questionText:
A  52-year-old  woman  who  has  had  low  back  pain  for  several  years  is  admitted  to  the  hospital  because  the  pain  has  
suddenly   worsened.   Her   current   medications   include   oxycodone,   amitriptyline,   perphenazine,   fluoxetine,   and   
trazodone.  The  patient's  body  weight  is  10%  below  ideal  weight.  Pupils  are  constricted  and  skin  turgor  is  poor.  She  
seems  sluggish  and  her  speech  is  slow.  Neurologic  examination  and  x-rays  of  the  lumbosacral  spine  disclose  no  
abnormalities.  If  a  medication  is  responsible  for  this  patient's  mental  condition,  the  medication  is  most  likely  to  be  
which of the following?

explanations.A: Amitriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant with anticholinergic and sedating properties that can cause confusion and sluggishness, but it characteristically causes dilated (not constricted) pupils due to its anticholinergic effects. The constricted pupils here point to an opioid rather than a TCA.
explanations.B: Fluoxetine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) that generally does not cause sedation, cognitive slowing, or miosis. SSRIs are not associated with constricted pupils or opioid-like CNS depression.
explanations.C: This is correct. Oxycodone is an opioid analgesic whose classic toxidrome includes miosis (constricted pupils), CNS depression (sluggishness, slow speech), and reduced body weight from chronic use. All of this patient's findings — constricted pupils, slow speech, and cognitive slowing — fit opioid toxicity or chronic opioid effect.
explanations.D: Perphenazine is a typical antipsychotic (phenothiazine) that can cause sedation and extrapyramidal effects but typically produces miosis via alpha-blockade only in overdose situations. The overall clinical picture here is more consistent with opioid effect than antipsychotic side effect.
explanations.E: Trazodone is a serotonin antagonist and reuptake inhibitor (SARI) used as a sedating antidepressant. While it can cause sedation, it does not specifically cause miosis or the degree of cognitive slowing described; it would not produce the classic opioid triad seen here.

questions.questionText:
A 65-year-old man is admitted to the hospital after he has an inferior wall myocardial infarction. Forty-eight hours later 
his vital signs are stable. ECG is shown. The most appropriate course of action is to do which of the following?
questions.imageAlt: Single-lead ECG strip showing inferior wall myocardial infarction changes

explanations.A: Atropine is used for symptomatic bradycardia (e.g., hemodynamically significant sinus bradycardia or Mobitz I AV block). In the setting of a stable, asymptomatic post-inferior MI arrhythmia with stable vital signs at 48 hours, atropine is not indicated.
explanations.B: Isoproterenol is a non-selective beta-agonist used for severe, hemodynamically unstable bradycardias or certain arrhythmias, but is not appropriate for a stable post-MI patient. Its use could increase myocardial oxygen demand and extend infarct size.
explanations.C: Synchronized cardioversion is indicated for hemodynamically unstable tachyarrhythmias (e.g., unstable atrial fibrillation, SVT, or VT with a pulse). This patient is hemodynamically stable 48 hours post-MI, so cardioversion is not warranted.
explanations.D: Pacemaker insertion is indicated for symptomatic high-degree AV block (complete heart block or Mobitz II) that is hemodynamically significant. Inferior MI commonly causes transient Mobitz I (Wenckebach) block, which is benign and self-limited, not requiring pacing.
explanations.E: This is correct. Inferior wall MI commonly causes transient AV conduction abnormalities (typically Mobitz I/Wenckebach) due to right coronary artery occlusion affecting the AV node. With stable vital signs at 48 hours, the appropriate management is observation, as this block usually resolves spontaneously without intervention.

questions.questionText:
A  healthy  2-year-old  African  American  child  is  brought  to  the  office  for  a  routine  well-child  visit.  The  child  was  
weaned  at  6  months  of  age  and  began  to  walk  at  10  months  of  age.  On  physical  examination,  she  has  mild  bowlegs  
(10-degree genu varum). The parents should be advised about which of the following?

choices.B: No treatment is needed at this time
choices.D: The child's intake of vitamin D should be increased
choices.E: A special exercise program is needed

explanations.A: Braces are indicated for progressive or severe physiologic genu varum, or for pathologic causes such as Blount disease. In an otherwise healthy 2-year-old with only 10 degrees of genu varum who is developing normally, bracing is premature and not evidence-based.
explanations.B: This is correct. Mild physiologic genu varum (bowlegs) is extremely common in toddlers up to age 2 and is a normal developmental variant. It typically self-corrects by age 3-4 without any intervention; a 10-degree varus in a healthy, normally developing 2-year-old requires no treatment.
explanations.C: Surgical correction is reserved for severe, progressive, or pathologic genu varum (such as significant Blount disease or rickets-related deformity) unresponsive to conservative management. It is entirely inappropriate for physiologic bowlegs in a 2-year-old.
explanations.D: Increased vitamin D intake would be appropriate if rickets were suspected. This healthy child with normal development, no dietary deficiency history, and only mild genu varum does not have evidence of rickets, making vitamin D supplementation unnecessary.
explanations.E: There is no evidence that special exercise programs correct physiologic genu varum. The condition self-resolves as normal developmental alignment progresses, and exercise programs would create unnecessary concern and burden for the family.

questions.questionText:
A 3-year-old child is brought to the emergency department by his teenage sister because he refuses to walk. The sister 
reports that she has been babysitting for 3 days while her parents are away on a trip and that the boy has been fussy for 
the  past  2  days.  Physical  examination  is  normal  except  for  painful  swelling  of  the  left  lower  leg.  In  addition  to  
radiography of the leg, you should obtain which of the following?

explanations.A: Abdominal ultrasonography would evaluate for intra-abdominal pathology but does not address the primary concern of non-accidental trauma (child abuse) presenting as an unexplained fracture in an unsupervised child. The leg injury and circumstances demand a broader evaluation.
explanations.B: CT of the abdomen has higher radiation exposure than a skeletal survey and is more appropriate when abdominal injury is specifically suspected. The clinical scenario of a young child with unexplained injury while under the care of a teenager calls for a comprehensive abuse workup, not targeted abdominal imaging.
explanations.C: CT of the head should be obtained if there are signs of head trauma (altered consciousness, neurologic findings, scalp injury). While shaken baby syndrome must be considered, the primary abuse workup tool for a child with a suspicious extremity fracture is a full skeletal survey.
explanations.D: This is correct. The circumstances — a 3-year-old with unexplained leg pain/swelling, cared for by a teenager while parents are away, refusing to walk — raise strong concern for non-accidental trauma (child abuse). A skeletal survey is the standard screening tool that detects occult fractures in various stages of healing across the body, which is the hallmark of physical abuse.
explanations.E: Serum lead concentration is appropriate when screening for lead poisoning, which can cause abdominal pain, irritability, and developmental issues. However, lead poisoning does not cause acute painful swelling of the lower leg, making this an irrelevant test in this acute presentation.

questions.questionText:
A  32-year-old  man  and  his  29-year-old  wife  come  to  the  office  for  evaluation  for  infertility.  The  wife's  gynecologist  
has reported that her anatomic and physiologic evaluation disclosed no abnormalities and that assessment of potential 
male  factors  is  needed.  The  husband  is  188  cm  (6  ft  3  in)  tall  with  fair  skin  and  little  facial  hair.  He  has  mild  
gynecomastia  and  small,  firm  testicles.  No  sperm  are  seen  on  semen  analysis.  Which  of  the  following  tests  is  most  
likely to establish the underlying cause of this couple's infertility?

choices.C: Serum follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone concentrations

explanations.A: This is correct. The clinical picture — tall stature, sparse facial hair, gynecomastia, small firm testes, and azoospermia — is classic for Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY). A karyotype from peripheral leukocytes is the definitive test to confirm this chromosomal diagnosis, which is the most common cause of male hypogonadism and infertility.
explanations.B: Serum estrogen and testosterone would show low testosterone and elevated estrogen (due to peripheral aromatization) in Klinefelter syndrome, but these findings are nonspecific and do not establish the underlying chromosomal cause. The karyotype is the definitive diagnostic test.
explanations.C: FSH and LH would be elevated (hypergonadotropic hypogonadism) in Klinefelter syndrome due to primary testicular failure, confirming primary hypogonadism. However, this does not establish the specific chromosomal etiology; the karyotype is needed for that.
explanations.D: Elevated serum prolactin can cause hypogonadism, azoospermia, and gynecomastia (prolactinoma). However, prolactinoma would not explain the tall stature, small firm testes, and sparse facial hair, which are specific phenotypic features of Klinefelter syndrome rather than hyperprolactinemia.
explanations.E: Testicular ultrasonography might show small, heterogeneous testes in Klinefelter syndrome but cannot establish the chromosomal diagnosis. It would be a secondary study at most and does not directly answer the cause of infertility in this phenotypically classic presentation.

questions.questionText:
A 58-year-old man comes to the office because of a lesion on his lower lip that developed 9 months ago. He has not 
seen a physician during the past 5 years and says, "My wife made me come to see you today." Physical examination of 
the lips discloses the findings shown in the photograph. The lower lip is fixed to the anterior aspect of the mandible. 
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.imageAlt: Clinical photograph of a lower lip lesion

explanations.A: Basal cell carcinoma rarely occurs on the lip and almost never presents as a fixed mass invading the mandible. BCC typically appears on sun-exposed skin of the face as a pearly, rolled-border lesion and has a much lower tendency for local bone invasion than squamous cell carcinoma.
explanations.B: Keratoacanthoma is a rapidly growing, self-limited squamous lesion that typically resolves within months. It presents as a dome-shaped nodule with a central keratin-filled crater; it does not invade underlying bone and has a much shorter natural history than this 9-month, fixed lesion.
explanations.C: Leukoplakia is a white mucosal patch that represents a precancerous lesion but is not itself a carcinoma. It does not form a fixed mass invading the mandible; the fixation to bone indicates invasive malignancy beyond the scope of leukoplakia alone.
explanations.D: Melanoma can occur on the lip but is far less common than squamous cell carcinoma at this site and is typically pigmented. While melanoma can invade deeply, SCC is overwhelmingly the most common malignancy of the lower lip, especially given the sun-exposed location and this clinical presentation.
explanations.E: This is correct. Squamous cell carcinoma is the most common malignancy of the lower lip, strongly associated with sun exposure (common in outdoor workers). A 9-month history of a growing lip lesion with fixation to the underlying mandible indicates invasive SCC with bone involvement, requiring urgent biopsy and oncologic management.

questions.questionText:
A  10-year-old  boy  is  brought  to  the  emergency  department  by  his  father  because  of  left  flank  pain  and  tenderness.  
About  4  hours  ago  the  boy  was  hit  hard  on  the  abdomen  during  a  karate  match.  Abdominal  CT  scan  shows  a  large  
splenic tear. In the pediatric intensive care unit his blood pressure is stabilized and his hematocrit is 45%. Which of the 
following is the most appropriate immediate step?

choices.A: Continue pulse and blood pressure monitoring
choices.E: Order radionuclide scan of the spleen

explanations.A: This is correct. In a hemodynamically stable pediatric patient with a splenic laceration (hematocrit 45%, stable blood pressure), non-operative management is the standard of care. Continuous monitoring of pulse and blood pressure allows detection of any deterioration while preserving the spleen, which is critical for immune function in children.
explanations.B: Splenectomy is reserved for hemodynamically unstable patients who fail resuscitation. This child has been stabilized with a normal hematocrit, so immediate splenectomy would be premature and would deprive him of splenic immune function, increasing lifetime risk of overwhelming post-splenectomy infection.
explanations.C: Diagnostic abdominal paracentesis is used when imaging is unavailable or equivocal (e.g., FAST exam or DPL). An abdominal CT has already confirmed the splenic tear, making paracentesis redundant and potentially harmful in a child with a known solid organ injury.
explanations.D: Intravenous urography evaluates the urinary tract and kidneys, not splenic injuries. It would be relevant if renal injury were suspected (e.g., hematuria), but it does not address the known splenic laceration and is not the appropriate next step.
explanations.E: Radionuclide scanning of the spleen is too slow and provides functional rather than anatomic detail. CT has already confirmed the diagnosis; repeat or alternative imaging is not needed when the clinical priority is monitoring the patient's hemodynamic stability.

questions.questionText:
A 42-year-old woman with a history of multiple sclerosis comes to the office because she had a sudden loss of vision 
in the right eye. She has no history of diplopia. External ocular movements are normal but funduscopic examination 
shows  pallor  of  the  optic  disk.  This  patient's  condition  is  most  likely  a  result  of  demyelination  of  which  of  the  
following?

explanations.A: Demyelination of the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) causes internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO), characterized by impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye with nystagmus in the contralateral eye on lateral gaze. This patient has normal extraocular movements, ruling out MLF involvement.
explanations.B: Oculomotor nerve (CN III) demyelination or compression causes ipsilateral ptosis, 'down-and-out' eye deviation, and a blown pupil. The patient has normal external ocular movements, making CN III involvement unlikely.
explanations.C: This is correct. Optic neuritis — demyelination of the optic nerve (CN II) — is the most common cause of sudden monocular visual loss in MS. The characteristic finding is optic disk pallor on funduscopy due to axonal loss, along with pain on eye movement; the normal extraocular movements confirm the lesion is in the afferent visual pathway, not the oculomotor system.
explanations.D: The trigeminal nerve (CN V) mediates facial sensation and mastication, not vision. Demyelination of CN V causes facial numbness or trigeminal neuralgia and would not produce monocular visual loss or optic disk pallor.
explanations.E: Demyelination of the visual cortex would cause a homonymous hemianopia (bilateral visual field defect) rather than complete monocular visual loss. The optic disk pallor on funduscopy directly implicates the optic nerve rather than a post-chiasmatic cortical lesion.

questions.questionText:
A  28-year-old  woman  who  is  known  to  be  HIV-positive  comes  to  the  emergency  department  because  of  a  1-week  
history  of  increasing  headaches,  right-sided  weakness,  and  disorientation.  A  generalized,  tonic-clonic  seizure  occurs  
shortly  after  admission.  Following  the  seizure,  vital  signs  are  normal.  There  is  no  nuchal  rigidity.  Funduscopic  
examination shows papilledema. There is also right hemiparesis and aphasia. Which of the following is the most likely 
diagnosis?

explanations.A: Meningioma is a benign intracranial tumor not associated with HIV status. It typically presents with a more gradual course and is not related to immunosuppression; it would be an incidental finding rather than an AIDS-defining illness in this clinical context.
explanations.B: Meningococcal meningitis presents with fever, nuchal rigidity, and petechial rash (meningococcemia). This patient has no nuchal rigidity and has focal neurologic deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia) with papilledema, which are more consistent with a space-occupying lesion than meningitis.
explanations.C: Neurosyphilis can cause dementia, meningitis, or tabes dorsalis in HIV patients, but it typically has a subacute-to-chronic course without focal mass lesions causing hemiparesis and seizures. Toxoplasmosis is far more common in HIV patients with CD4 <100 cells/µL presenting with focal neurologic deficits.
explanations.D: This is correct. Toxoplasma gondii reactivation is the most common cause of focal encephalitis in AIDS patients with CD4 counts below 100 cells/µL. It classically presents with headache, fever, focal neurologic deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia), seizures, and ring-enhancing lesions on CT/MRI — all consistent with this case.
explanations.E: Tuberculous meningitis in HIV patients typically presents with subacute meningitis (fever, headache, nuchal rigidity, cranial nerve palsies) rather than acute focal deficits from mass lesions. The absence of nuchal rigidity and the pattern of focal deficits favors toxoplasmosis over TB meningitis.

questions.questionText:
A  5-year-old  girl  with  a  lumbar  myelomeningocele  is  brought  to  the  office  by  her  mother  for  a  periodic  health  
evaluation. The girl has little motor function and no sensation below the waist. She has a neurogenic bladder requiring 
intermittent  catheterization,  and  she  also  requires  daily  suppositories  to  aid  in  bowel  movements.  She  does  not  have  
hydrocephalus but can walk only with the aid of braces and crutches. "I am pleased with how well she is doing," says 
the mother, "but I am so worried about what might happen to her later on." Which of the following is the most likely 
late complication in this patient?

explanations.A: Carcinoma of the bladder is not a recognized late complication of myelomeningocele. While neurogenic bladder increases the risk of urinary tract infections, the primary long-term renal risk is from recurrent infections and high-pressure bladder leading to hydronephrosis and renal failure.
explanations.B: COPD is caused by smoking and environmental exposures and is not a direct complication of lumbar myelomeningocele. This child's myelomeningocele does not impair respiratory function (it is a lumbar-level lesion, not thoracic or cervical), making COPD an unlikely late complication.
explanations.C: Cor pulmonale (right heart failure from pulmonary hypertension) is associated with chronic lung disease or sleep-disordered breathing and is not a recognized late complication of lumbar-level myelomeningocele. The child's respiratory function is not compromised by this lesion.
explanations.D: This is correct. The most significant late complication in myelomeningocele patients is end-stage renal disease, resulting from neurogenic bladder dysfunction. Chronic urinary tract infections, vesicoureteral reflux, and obstructive uropathy from the neurogenic bladder progressively damage the kidneys; renal failure is the leading cause of death in adults with myelomeningocele.
explanations.E: Osteomyelitis of the femoral head is not a characteristic complication of myelomeningocele. Although pressure ulcers and fractures can occur due to lack of sensation and mobility, osteomyelitis of the femoral head specifically is not a well-established late complication of this condition.

questions.questionText:
A 26-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his family because he has been telling them that he is 
being followed by gangsters and that they are going to kill him. The family states that he has a history of drug abuse. 
Temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 160/95 mm Hg. His pupils are dilated. The 
remainder  of  the  physical  examination  discloses  no  abnormalities.  Which  of  the  following  drugs  most  likely  caused  
this reaction?

explanations.A: Alcohol intoxication causes CNS depression, slurred speech, and ataxia, while withdrawal can cause agitation and hallucinations. However, alcohol would not cause dilated pupils, hypertension, and tachycardia together with paranoid delusions; the sympathomimetic toxidrome here is inconsistent with alcohol.
explanations.B: This is correct. Cocaine is a sympathomimetic that causes release and reuptake inhibition of catecholamines, resulting in the classic toxidrome seen here: dilated pupils, hypertension, tachycardia, fever, and paranoid delusions. Cocaine-induced psychosis with persecutory delusions is well recognized and is the most consistent diagnosis given the entire clinical picture.
explanations.C: Diazepam is a benzodiazepine CNS depressant that causes sedation, respiratory depression, and ataxia. It would not produce the sympathomimetic findings (dilated pupils, hypertension, tachycardia) or paranoid psychosis seen in this patient.
explanations.D: Heroin (opioid) toxicity presents with the classic triad of miosis (pinpoint pupils), CNS depression, and respiratory depression. This patient has dilated pupils, hypertension, and agitation — the opposite of an opioid toxidrome.
explanations.E: Methaqualone (Quaalude) is a sedative-hypnotic that causes CNS depression similar to barbiturates. It would produce sedation and respiratory depression rather than the hyperadrenergic state with paranoid psychosis seen in this patient.

questions.questionText:
Several patients with hypertension whom you have treated for many years have recently had strokes. You are frustrated 
by this outcome and review the literature on the efficacy of antihypertensive treatments in preventing stroke. A large, 
multicenter,  randomized  trial  shows  that  a  particular  antihypertensive  medication  lowers  the  5-year  risk  for  stroke  
from  8  per  1000  patients  to  6  per  1000  patients,  providing  a  relative  risk  reduction  of  25%.  Based  on  this  study,  the  
number of patients with hypertension who must be treated to prevent one stroke is which of the following?

explanations.A: An NNT of 4 would imply an absolute risk reduction of 25%, which would be an extraordinarily large treatment effect. The absolute risk reduction here is only 2 per 1000 (8 minus 6 per 1000), so NNT = 1/0.002 = 500, not 4.
explanations.B: An NNT of 75 would correspond to an absolute risk reduction of approximately 1.3%, which is too large given the data. The confusion arises from mistaking the relative risk reduction (25%) for the absolute risk reduction; the actual ARR is much smaller.
explanations.C: An NNT of 250 would correspond to an absolute risk reduction of 0.4%. This is incorrect; the actual absolute risk reduction is 2 per 1000 (0.2%), giving an NNT of 500.
explanations.D: This is correct. The number needed to treat (NNT) = 1 / absolute risk reduction (ARR). The ARR = 8/1000 − 6/1000 = 2/1000 = 0.002. Therefore NNT = 1/0.002 = 500. The relative risk reduction of 25% is misleading without considering the low baseline event rate.
explanations.E: An NNT of 2000 would correspond to an absolute risk reduction of only 0.05%, which is too small. The actual ARR is 2 per 1000 patients (0.2%), making NNT = 500, not 2000.

questions.setVignette:
A  48-year-old  Native  American  construction  worker,  who  sustained  a  comminuted  fracture  of  his  left  tibia  and  fibula  
4  months  ago,  is  transferred  from  the  rehabilitation  facility  to  the  emergency  department  because  of  a  3-hour  history  of  
dyspnea  and  chest  pain.  During  the  past  3  months  since  sustaining  the  fracture,  he  has  resided  in  the  rehabilitation  facility  
with his left lower extremity fully immobilized. He now describes an aching discomfort over the right superior anterior chest 
and the right scapula posteriorly. Family history is strongly positive for heart disease.
questions.questionText:
In questioning the patient further, an important point in the history would be the relationship of the pain to which of the 
following?

explanations.A: Pleuritic chest pain does not typically change with positional shifts (which is characteristic of pericardial pain, worse when lying flat and relieved by leaning forward). In the context of a patient with a recent lower extremity fracture and new-onset dyspnea with chest pain, pulmonary embolus is the primary concern.
explanations.B: This is correct. The patient has classic risk factors for pulmonary embolism (prolonged immobility from tibial/fibular fracture). Pleuritic chest pain — sharp pain that worsens with deep inspiration and is relieved by shallow breathing — is a hallmark of PE, particularly when pulmonary infarction has occurred causing pleural irritation.
explanations.C: Pain related to eating suggests a gastrointestinal etiology (e.g., peptic ulcer, esophageal spasm, pancreatitis). While abdominal pathology can cause dyspnea and chest discomfort, this relationship is not useful in evaluating the primary concern of pulmonary embolism in this high-risk patient.
explanations.D: Pain related to swallowing (odynophagia/dysphagia) suggests esophageal pathology. This is not relevant to the evaluation of pulmonary embolism and would not help characterize the pleuritic quality of pain expected in PE with infarction.
explanations.E: Pain worsened by walking might suggest musculoskeletal or vascular etiology in other contexts, but the patient is likely immobilized due to his fracture. Relationship of chest pain to respiratory effort (deep breathing) is the key historical point in distinguishing pleuritic from other types of chest pain.

questions.questionText: The presence of a right pleural friction rub in this patient would suggest which of the following?

explanations.A: A pericardial friction rub, not a pleural friction rub, is heard in pericarditis. A pleural rub is a coarser, leathery sound heard over the lung fields that changes with breathing but not with a held breath, distinguishing it from pericardial rub; pericarditis would not cause a pleural rub.
explanations.B: Pneumonia causes fever, productive cough, and consolidation on exam, and can produce a pleural rub if pleuritis is present. However, in the specific context of this post-fracture patient with high PE risk and acute dyspnea, a pleural rub is more specifically associated with pulmonary infarction.
explanations.C: Pneumothorax causes decreased or absent breath sounds with hyperresonance on the affected side. A friction rub would not be expected with pneumothorax, as the pleural surfaces separate rather than rub together when the pleural space is filled with air.
explanations.D: This is correct. A pleural friction rub in the setting of pulmonary embolism indicates pulmonary infarction, where infarcted lung tissue causes inflammation of the adjacent pleura, producing the characteristic leathery, creaking rub. PE without infarction does not cause pleural inflammation and thus would not produce a friction rub.
explanations.E: Pulmonary embolism without infarction does not cause a pleural friction rub because the pleura remain uninflamed when lung tissue is not infarcted. The rub specifically indicates that infarction has occurred, making this choice incorrect for the finding described.

questions.questionText:
A  68-year-old  woman,  who  underwent  flexible  sigmoidoscopy  6  hours  ago  in  the  office  as  part  of  routine  screening,  
returns to the office because of left lower quadrant abdominal pain, fever, nausea, and vomiting. During the procedure, 
a  3-cm  polyp  was  found  in  the  sigmoid  colon  and  was  removed.  Vital  signs  now  are  temperature  38.1°C  (100.6°F),  
pulse  110/min,  respirations  26/min,  and  blood  pressure  120/60  mm  Hg.  Abdominal  examination  discloses  bowel  
sounds,  and  tenderness  and  guarding  in  the  left  lower  quadrant.  Rectal  examination  shows  no  stool  and  tenderness  
only superiorly. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.A: Obtain angiography to rule out intestinal ischemia
choices.B: Obtain immediate consultation with a surgeon
choices.C: Pass a soft rubber rectal tube under fluoroscopy
choices.D: Repeat the flexible sigmoidoscopy to evaluate the operative site
choices.E: Start hydrocortisone, intravenously, to decrease any inflammatory response

explanations.A: Angiography for intestinal ischemia would be indicated if mesenteric ischemia were suspected (e.g., acute onset pain with atrial fibrillation, diffuse abdominal pain out of proportion to exam). A post-polypectomy complication with localized left lower quadrant findings and peritoneal signs is more consistent with perforation, not ischemia.
explanations.B: This is correct. The clinical presentation — fever, tachycardia, tachypnea, left lower quadrant pain with tenderness and guarding 6 hours after sigmoid polypectomy — is classic for colonic perforation, a known complication of polypectomy. Immediate surgical consultation is mandatory for likely bowel perforation requiring urgent operative intervention.
explanations.C: Passing a rectal tube under fluoroscopy might be used to decompress colonic volvulus or pseudo-obstruction. In suspected post-polypectomy perforation with peritoneal signs, any rectal instrumentation could worsen the perforation and is contraindicated.
explanations.D: Repeat sigmoidoscopy after polypectomy perforation would introduce air and pressure into an already perforated colon, potentially worsening contamination and injury. Endoscopy is contraindicated when perforation is suspected.
explanations.E: Hydrocortisone has no role in the management of colonic perforation and could actually mask signs of sepsis while impairing healing. Anti-inflammatory therapy is inappropriate when the primary problem is mechanical perforation requiring surgical repair.
